visits.text: Augsburg, founded in 15 BCE by Emperor Augustus's legions, is one of Germany's oldest cities. During the Renaissance, it became one of Europe's financial capitals: the Fugger and Welser dynasties of merchants and bankers lent money to Emperors Maximilian I and Charles V. It was in Augsburg that the Augsburg Confession, the founding charter of Lutheranism, was read in 1530, and where the Peace of Augsburg was signed in 1555, officially recognizing the coexistence of religions within the Empire. The city is also the birthplace of Leopold Mozart, the composer's father, and writer Bertolt Brecht. It's the only city in Germany with its own public holiday: on August 8th, the Peace Festival commemorates the end of Protestant oppression after the Thirty Years' War.

The Fuggerei, founded in 1521 by Jakob Fugger—considered the richest man of his time—is the world's oldest social housing complex still in operation. Behind its walls and blue-and-white-striped portal, 67 ochre-façaded houses contain 142 apartments of 60 square meters each. The annual rent, fixed in 1521 at one Rhenish guilder, has never changed: today it equals €0.88. In return, residents must be Catholic, native to Augsburg, in financial need, and recite three daily prayers for the founders. A curfew remains in effect at 10 PM. Mozart's great-great-grandfather, mason Franz Mozart, lived here from 1681 to 1694—a commemorative plaque marks the spot. A model apartment and a 1943 bunker can be visited.

The Town Hall (Rathaus), designed by architect Elias Holl and completed in 1620, is considered the most important secular Renaissance building north of the Alps. It was the world's first building with more than six stories. Its Golden Hall (Goldener Saal), finished in 1643, impresses with its 14-meter-high coffered ceiling covered in gold leaf, murals, and marble floor. Everything was destroyed by 1944 bombings but faithfully reconstructed. Right next door, the Perlach Tower (Perlachturm), standing 70 meters tall, offers panoramas across the entire city and, on clear days, to the Alps.

Stroll down Maximilianstraße, the city's finest avenue, laid out along the ancient Roman Via Claudia Augusta. Three monumental bronze fountains punctuate the promenade: the Augustus Fountain (1588-1594) by Dutch sculptor Hubert Gerhard, the Mercury Fountain (1596-1599), and the Hercules Fountain (1597-1600) by Adriaen de Vries. These fountains are part of Augsburg's water management system, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2019—an ensemble of 22 hydraulic monuments spanning more than 700 years of history. At No. 36, the Fugger Palace features a Renaissance arcaded courtyard; at No. 46, the Schaezler Palace (1770) houses the most important gallery of German Baroque art and a Rococo ballroom.

Augsburg Cathedral (Dom), whose oldest parts date back to 994, blends Romanesque and Gothic architecture. It preserves the world's oldest known figurative stained glass windows, dating from the 11th century: the Prophets—Daniel, Jonah, Hosea, Moses, and David—installed in the nave's south windows. The Romanesque bronze door, considered the cathedral's oldest work of art, is displayed in the adjoining diocesan museum.

activities.text: Settle into one of the downtown breweries to try Käsespätzle, gratinated fresh cheese noodles, a Swabian specialty from Augsburg. The city, on the border between Bavaria and Swabia, offers cuisine drawing from both traditions—complete your meal with Leberkäse (meatloaf) or Zwetschgendatschi (plum tart).

Attend a performance at the Augsburger Puppenkiste, the famous marionette theater founded in 1943. Housed in the former Holy Spirit Hospital, this string puppet theater stages fairy tales for children as well as adult productions, with an attention to detail that has made it renowned throughout Germany.

Germany's Romantic Road: Fairytale Castles and Medieval Towns of Bavaria

visits.text: Augsburg is Germany's second-oldest city after Trier, founded in 15 BC by Roman legions—it takes its name from Emperor Augustus. Its financial grandeur is visible on Maximilianstrasse, an Italian-inspired Renaissance boulevard flanked by three bronze fountains representing Augustus, Mercury, and Hercules, designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2019. These fountains, erected around 1600, are the first monumental bronze works to have been cast in Augsburg.

The Fuggerei is Augsburg's most singular site: founded in 1516 by banker Jakob Fugger the Rich (the world's richest man in his time, creditor to Hapsburg emperors and the Vatican), it's the world's oldest social housing complex in continuous use. The quarter comprises 67 houses and 147 apartments across 8 streets. Tenants still pay an annual rent of 1 euro in exchange for three daily prayers for the Fugger souls. Mozart's great-great-grandfather, Franz Mozart, was one of its most famous residents.

The Town Hall (Rathaus), built between 1615 and 1624 by architect Elias Holl, is one of Germany's finest Renaissance buildings. Its interior is dominated by the Goldene Saal (Golden Hall), a 33-meter-long reception hall with gilded coffered ceiling. The adjacent Perlachturm tower (70 meters) dates from the 11th century and offers views over the entire city.

Augsburg Cathedral (Dom) dates to the 9th century and contains Germany's oldest figural stained glass windows, dating from the mid-12th century—prophets depicted in colored glass. The cathedral also houses four paintings by Hans Holbein the Elder on the aisle columns.

activities.text: Visit the Schaezlerpalais, a baroque palace built in 1770, which houses a rococo ballroom with Tiepolo-style frescoes and the Staatsgalerie—a collection of Renaissance and medieval paintings including Dürer's portrait of Jakob Fugger the Rich.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: Arrive via the right bank of the Rhine—the road follows the river between Rüdesheim and Bacharach, offering a succession of terraced vineyards, perched castle ruins, and half-timbered villages characteristic of the UNESCO World Heritage Upper Middle Rhine Valley. Before reaching Bacharach, stop at Rüdesheim for the Drosselgasse, a 144-meter cobbled alley entirely lined with wine taverns and beer gardens. The wine served is mainly Rheingau Riesling.

Ride the cable car (Seilbahn) up to the Niederwald Monument, a bronze statue of Germania built between 1877 and 1883 from sculptor Johann Schilling's design, commemorating the 1870-71 victory and German Empire unification. The ensemble measures 38 meters in height and offers panoramic views over the Rhine and Rheingau vineyards.

Enter Bacharach through the ancient fortified gates. The 14th-century ramparts are among the best preserved in the entire Rhine valley. Walk along the rampart path to see the slate roofs, vineyards, and Rhine below from above. The village is first mentioned in a document dated 923; its name evokes Bacchus, god of wine, whose cult already existed in Celtic and Roman times.

The Gothic ruins of Wernerkapelle, halfway up the slope between the village and Stahleck Castle. The chapel was begun in 1289 in memory of Werner of Oberwesel, a 16-year-old boy murdered in 1287. Its construction was never completed, interrupted by pogroms against the local Jewish community, falsely accused of the murder. A plaque today commemorates this past. The partially preserved Gothic structure remains striking despite the centuries.

Stahleck Castle dominates Bacharach from a ridge 160 meters above the Rhine. Its first mention dates to 1135. Destroyed by French troops during the War of the Palatine Succession in 1689, it was reconstructed in 1925 and now houses a youth hostel. Access to the inner courtyard is free—from the terrace, the view over the Rhine and valley meanders is one of the finest in the area.

activities.text: Riesling tasting at one of the village wine estates. Bacharach has produced wine since Roman times. Legend has it that Pope Pius II had it shipped by cart all the way to Rome.

Short Rhine cruise from Bacharach pier to Kaub, to see from the water the fortress of Pfalzgrafenstein perched on an island in the middle of the river—a former medieval customs post still intact. Round trip about 1h15.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: The Stiftsruine of Bad Hersfeld is the world's largest Romanesque church ruin, stretching over 100 meters in length with a transept span exceeding 50 meters. The Benedictine abbey was founded in 769 by Lullus, Archbishop of Mainz, on a site where the monk Sturmius had settled as a hermit in 736. Charlemagne made it an imperial abbey that served for centuries as a pillar of royal power on the eastern frontier of the Frankish kingdom. The church was destroyed by the French in 1761 during the Seven Years' War, when it was being used as a powder magazine. The Romanesque Saint Catherine's Tower, dating from 1120, still stands and houses the Lullusglocke—a bell dated 1038, Germany's oldest dated bell still in use. Since 1951, the ruins have hosted the Bad Hersfeld Festival, one of Germany's most important theater festivals, every summer.

The historic center of Bad Hersfeld, a Hessian spa town, has preserved a fabric of 15th and 16th-century half-timbered houses. The sacristan's house, dating from 1452, is the town's oldest. The Stadtkirche (town church) and the former buildings of the free imperial city form a cohesive ensemble around the pedestrian center.

activities.text: Attend the Bad Hersfeld Festival, an annual theater festival held each summer in the Stiftsruine ruins since 1951. The open-air stage, set against nine-century-old Romanesque walls, hosts large-scale productions (Shakespeare, Schiller, musicals) with up to 1,500 spectators per evening.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: The Grosser Feldberg peaks at 879 meters, the highest point in the Taunus and the state of Hesse. On clear days, the summit view extends to the Alps and Cologne Cathedral. The ascent through beech and spruce forests of the Naturpark Hochtaunus offers a dramatic shift from the Main River plain. Several marked trails lead to the summit from surrounding villages.

The Naturpark Hochtaunus covers 240,000 hectares of forests, valleys, and ridges between the Main and Lahn rivers. It's one of Hesse's largest nature parks. The trail network exceeds 3,000 km. The vegetation is typically Rhenish: oak, beech, and hornbeam groves on the slopes, with some glacial relict species on the ridges.

The historic center of Bad Homburg vor der Höhe retains its 17th-century Schloss—one of the few remaining residences of the Landgraves of Hesse-Homburg still standing—with its medieval white tower that has become the town's emblem. The Kurpark, landscaped in English style with century-old avenues, ranks among Germany's finest spa parks. In the 19th century, Bad Homburg was a fashionable resort for European nobility: Tsar Nicholas II, King Edward VII, and Emperor Wilhelm II were regular visitors. The first Monte Carlo casino is said to have been founded by businessmen who learned the trade here.

The Roman fort of Saalburg, in the Taunus a few kilometers from Bad Homburg, is the only fully reconstructed fort along the Germanic Limes. Built in the late 1st century to house a cohort of 500 soldiers, it was abandoned during the Alamannic raids of 258 AD. Serious excavations began in 1853. In 1897, Wilhelm II—passionate about Roman history—ordered its complete reconstruction on the original foundations, an unprecedented project for a military installation. The site has been part of the UNESCO Upper Germanic-Raetian Limes inscription since 2005. The archaeological museum displays weapons, tools, and everyday objects found on site.

activities.text: Spa session at the Taunus Therme in Bad Homburg, a complex featuring over 1,400 m² of pools and eight themed saunas. Bad Homburg's springs have been used therapeutically since the 17th century.

Visit the Hessenpark open-air museum in Neu-Anspach, about ten kilometers from Bad Homburg. Over 100 historic buildings—half-timbered houses, barns, mills, workshops—have been dismantled and reassembled across 70 hectares. The site offers a tangible picture of Hessian rural architecture from the 16th to 19th centuries.

Hike the Limes trail from Saalburg: portions of the fortifications (reconstructed ditch and palisade) remain visible in the Taunus landscape. A 2.4 km circular trail around the fort runs along preserved sections and marks the bases of former watchtowers.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Stroll along the graduation wall of Bad Kösen, an impressive wooden structure where salt water flows over blackthorn bundles. This traditional spa installation creates iodine-enriched air with invigorating properties, perpetuating a centuries-old wellness tradition. Various viewpoints over the spa town reveal a hilly landscape typical of the Saale-Unstrut region.

Discover this charming spa town nestled in the Saale valley, at the gateway to Germany's northernmost wine-growing region. The Belle Époque villa architecture testifies to the golden age of German spa culture in the 19th century. The surrounding vine-planted hills offer bucolic panoramas over this preserved landscape.

activities.text: Walk through the spa park gardens and discover the spa architecture

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Bad Langensalza owes its medieval prosperity to woad trade - a blue dye it shared with only four other Thuringian cities - enabling burghers to build grand churches and travertine ramparts. Later, the exploitation of mineral springs made it a spa town. Today, the city is known for its ten themed parks and gardens aligned along the spa promenade. The Japanese garden «Kofuku no Niwa» («garden of happiness») combines two classical pavilions, a koi carp pond, a dry garden, a bamboo grove, and cherry trees that blossom in spring.

Bad Langensalza's rose garden is one of Thuringia's most significant. It showcases collections of antique and modern roses organized in themed beds, complemented by a small rose museum tracing this flower's cultural history.

The arboretum gathers rare or spectacular trees and shrubs, native and exotic species, planted in thematic collections. Adjacent, the botanical garden displays medicinal and officinal plants recalling the town's spa tradition.

Bad Langensalza's old town is one of Thuringia's best-preserved: an almost entirely conserved medieval wall, half-timbered houses from the 15th and 16th centuries restored since 1990, and several grand churches testifying to the past wealth of the woad trade.

activities.text: Relaxation at the Friederiken Therme, a thermal spa fed by Bad Langensalza's sulphurous and saline springs. The complex features indoor and outdoor pools and several themed saunas.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Take the Schwarzwaldhochstrasse (B500) to reach Baden-Baden via the heights—60 kilometers of forests and ridges, this is the panoramic route through the northern Black Forest. Take it from the south when arriving from Triberg.

Descend to the old town to see the Roman bath ruins: beneath the market square, excavations discovered in 1847 unearthed baths built between 213 and 217 AD for Emperor Caracalla. Baden-Baden's springs gush from 2,000 meters deep at temperatures between 56 and 68 degrees—800,000 liters per day emerge from twelve artesian springs aged 12,000 to 17,000 years. The Romans called the town Aquae Aureliae.

Book a session at the Friedrichsbad, built between 1869 and 1877 by architect Karl Dernfeld. Inspired by Roman baths and Irish baths, it offers 17 thermal stations under a 17.5-meter dome. Mark Twain visited and wrote: "After 10 minutes you forget time; after 20 minutes, the world." The bathing tradition here is mixed and clothing-free.

End the day with a walk along the Lichtentaler Allee, which follows the Oos River for 2.3 kilometers between the town center and the Cistercian abbey of Lichtenthal founded in 1245. This tree-lined avenue of centuries-old trees was in the 19th century the open-air salon of Europe—Napoleon III, Queen Victoria, Dostoevsky and Brahms all stayed here.

activities.text: The modern Caracalla Therme, opened in 1985 over 5,000 square meters, allows you to enjoy the thermal waters with swimwear. Several pools at temperatures between 18 and 38 degrees, outdoor pool open year-round, saunas and steam baths. More accessible than the Friedrichsbad, with or without reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Marvel at Bamberg, one of Germany's most beautiful cities, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This millennial city miraculously spared by bombing has more than 2,300 buildings classified as historic monuments. Built on seven hills like Rome, it harmoniously blends medieval, Baroque and Rococo architecture in an ensemble of exceptional coherence.

Photograph the Altes Rathaus, the old town hall built on an artificial island in the middle of the Regnitz River, Bavaria's most iconic monument. Its trompe-l'oeil frescoes adorning the half-timbered facades create the illusion of three-dimensional sculptures. According to legend, when the bishop refused to cede land, the burghers created this island to build their town hall.

Visit the four-towered Imperial Cathedral, a Romanesque masterpiece housing the tomb of Emperor Henry II and the famous Bamberg Horseman, the first life-size equestrian statue of the Middle Ages. Little Venice, the former fishermen's quarter with colorful houses reflected in the river, completes this enchanting picture.

activities.text: Tasting of Rauchbier, the smoked beer that is a unique local specialty in the world

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: The Bastei is Saxon Switzerland's iconic rock formation—sandstone columns sculpted by erosion over a million years, dominating the Elbe by 194 meters from a plateau at 305 meters altitude. The Bastei Bridge, built in sandstone in 1851 to replace a first wooden bridge from 1826, links several of these rocky pinnacles over 76.5 meters with its seven arches. Caspar David Friedrich painted the rocks as early as 1822. In season, crowds are dense from 10 a.m.—arrive early for the best views over the Elbe's meanders.

The ruins of Neurathen Castle, on the very site of the Bastei rocks, are the vestiges of Saxon Switzerland's largest rock fortress. Built by the Sorbs at the top of the ridge over 700 meters, it was conquered and burned in 1469. Metal walkways now allow circulation between the rock crevices and ruins.

activities.text: Hike from the village of Rathen to the Bastei Bridge and surrounding rocks. The departure is from the right bank of the Elbe after crossing by ferry (a few minutes), then a climb of about 40 minutes through the forest. Several trails allow for varied returns.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Bautzen perches on a meander of the Spree, surrounded by its medieval towers—eighteen are still standing. It's the cultural capital of the Sorbs, Germany's smallest ethnic minority: about 60,000 people still speak Upper Sorbian in this Upper Lusatia region. Their migrations from Eastern Europe brought them here in the 6th century. Bilingual German-Sorbian signs line the entire city. The Sorbian Museum traces their history, traditional costumes, and living culture.

St. Peter's Cathedral is Germany's oldest and largest simultaneous church—shared between Catholics and Protestants. Since the Reformation in 1524, the choir has belonged to Catholics and the nave to Protestants, separated by a rood screen grille. Each of the two denominations celebrates its services according to its own calendar. The Gothic part dates back to the 13th century; the Baroque dome of the tower was added in 1664 after the fire of 1634. The Baroque marble altar in Saxon marble (1723) is topped by a painting by Giovanni Antonio Pellegrini.

The Reichenturm, Bautzen's leaning tower, tilts 1.44 meters from the vertical—a foundation defect from the 14th century that was never corrected. It's visible from the market square and offers a beautiful view over the roofs from its summit.

activities.text: Walk on the old town ramparts above the Spree. The path runs along several medieval towers and offers views over the river and gardens below.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Behold the Königssee, the absolute jewel of German lakes, set between 1,800-meter vertical cliffs in the heart of Berchtesgaden National Park. This fjord-like lake with exceptionally pure emerald-green waters can only be explored by silent electric boats, perpetuating a century-old ecological tradition.

Navigate to St. Bartholomew's pilgrimage church, a Baroque gem with red onion domes perched on a peninsula at the foot of the vertiginous Watzmann wall. The legendary echo of the captain's trumpet resonating seven times against the cliffs is a magical moment of the crossing. This site of surreal beauty ranks among Germany's most photographed.

Hike in Berchtesgaden National Park, Germany's only Alpine national park, classified as a UNESCO biosphere reserve. This natural sanctuary is home to chamois, marmots and golden eagles in a setting of jagged peaks, tumultuous waterfalls and flowering alpine meadows. The town of Berchtesgaden itself charms with its preserved Bavarian authenticity.

activities.text: Electric boat cruise on Königssee

visits.text: Discovery of Berlin's historic center, including the Brandenburg Gate and the Holocaust Memorial.
Bike ride in Tiergarten park and on the former Tempelhof airport.
Visit of the East Side Gallery and exploration of the Friedrichshain district.
Return stage to Berlin, with possibility to stop in Waren on Lake Müritz for a walk on the shores.
Discovery of Neustrelitz and its baroque gardens.

activities.text: Stroll in the trendy Neukölln district and tasting of Middle Eastern specialties.
Discovery of the Sunday flea markets at Mauerpark and Arkonaplatz.
Last evening in Berlin for a nighttime stroll around Alexanderplatz.

Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: The Blautopf ('blue pot') is Germany's second-largest karst spring, located at the edge of the Swabian Jura 16 km west of Ulm. The funnel-shaped basin reaches 21 meters deep; its intense turquoise-blue color results from light scattering by limestone particles suspended in extremely clear water. During heavy rainfall, the water 'boils' as it surges under pressure from the underground karst network of the Blauhöhlen caves, explored for over 18 km by cave divers since 1985—several divers have lost their lives here, prohibiting access to non-professionals. Swabian legend tells of a goblin pouring a bucket of ink each morning to maintain the color; another claims a water nymph steals the lead ropes that curious visitors attempt to lower to measure the depth—hence the nearby rock nicknamed Klötzle Blei ('little bit of lead').

The Benedictine monastery of Blaubeuren, founded in 1085 on the heights above the Blautopf, is one of Baden-Württemberg's best-preserved Romanesque monastic complexes. The abbey church houses a 15th-century Gothic altarpiece and remarkable carved choir stalls. The Hammerschmiede (hammer forge), powered by waters from the Blautopf, is visible at the site entrance; a film there documents the exploration dives into the caves.

The Museum of Prehistory in Blaubeuren (Urgeschichtliches Museum) preserves pieces discovered in the surrounding Swabian Jura caves—including replicas of the Venus of Hohle Fels, the ivory mammoth figurine discovered in 2008 by archaeologist Nicholas Conard in the Hohle Fels cave at Schelklingen (7 km away). Standing 6 cm tall and dating between 35,000 and 40,000 years ago, it's the oldest known figurative sculpture in the world. The same cave also yielded flutes made from vulture bone and ivory dating to approximately 35,000 years ago—among the oldest known musical instruments.

activities.text: Guided tour of Hohle Fels cave at Schelklingen (7 km from Blaubeuren). The cave is open for guided tours Wednesday through Sunday from May to October. The site sits at 616 meters altitude in the Swabian Jura; excavations begun in 1870 have uncovered Upper Paleolithic artifacts across several meters of sediment.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: Bremen's Marktplatz is one of the best-preserved medieval ensembles in northern Germany. The town hall (Rathaus), built in the 15th century in Gothic style then remodeled in Weser Renaissance in the 17th century, and the Roland statue erected in 1404—symbol of municipal freedoms—have been UNESCO-listed since 2004. Bremen had joined the Hanseatic League in 1358; much of its prosperity came from the 17th century onward through coffee and cotton trade.

St. Peter's Cathedral (Dom St. Petri) was founded in 789 by Willehad, Bremen's first bishop—who died that same year. The current sandstone edifice was built starting in 1042 and remodeled several times. Its two green-roofed towers dominate the Marktplatz. In the crypt, eight naturally preserved mummies from the 17th and 18th centuries, maintained by particularly dry conditions.

The Schnoor quarter is the old fishermen's district, whose 15th- and 16th-century half-timbered houses have remained virtually intact. Its alleyways so narrow you can touch both facades by stretching your arms now house galleries, artisans, and cafes.

Böttcherstrasse is a 100-meter street rebuilt in the 1920s in expressionist style, mixing carved red bricks and decorative art—commissioned by a coffee merchant enriched in the Brazilian trade. At the entrance, a gold relief depicting the archangel Michael slaying a dragon.

The Kunsthalle Bremen, founded in 1849, is one of Germany's oldest art museums. Its collection covers Flemish and Dutch painting, German Romanticism, French Impressionism—Monet, Pissarro—and modern art. It holds over 200,000 drawings and prints, one of the most important collections of this type in Europe.

activities.text: Stroll along the Schlachte, the riverside promenade along the Weser that runs the entire length of the old town. Terraces and moored boats—very lively on summer evenings.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Conquer the Brocken, Saxony-Anhalt's highest point and the highest mountain in northern Germany at 1,141 meters elevation. This mythical summit, shrouded in mist and legend, is the gathering place of witches during Walpurgis Night according to Germanic folklore. On clear days, panoramas from the summit extend up to 200 kilometers, embracing the northern plains and neighboring massifs.

Board the Harzer Brockenbahn steam train, one of Germany's last narrow-gauge railways still in regular service. This historic line climbs the slopes of the Brocken through fir forests and highland peat bog landscapes in a nostalgic atmosphere. The 90-minute train journey constitutes an experience as memorable as the destination itself.

Discover Heinrich Heine Rock, named in honor of the famous poet who climbed the Brocken in 1820 and immortalized this experience in his travel narrative. Hiking trails wind through a subarctic landscape unique in central Germany, where stunted vegetation testifies to the extreme climatic conditions. The delicious Brockensplitter chocolates are the gourmet reward for this ascent.

activities.text: Hiking or steam train journey to the Brocken summit

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Celle's old town (Altstadt) boasts over 500 half-timbered houses designated as historical monuments, the oldest dating back to the 15th century. The town's name derives from the Latin cella—a small monastic chamber—recalling an ancient monastery established on the site. First mentioned in 985 as Kellu, Celle served as the residence of the Dukes of Brunswick-Lüneburg from 1378 to 1705. The carved beams above doorways once indicated the occupants' trades.

The Hoppener Haus, built in 1532, stands among Germany's most lavishly decorated half-timbered houses. Its six-level facade is covered with carvings depicting devils, jesters, planetary gods, mythical creatures, and reptiles. Across the street, two lampposts equipped with speakers recount local anecdotes in both standard German and Low Saxon dialect—they activate automatically as you pass.

Celle Castle (Schloss Celle) dominates the banks of the Aller River from a gentle hill surrounded by moats and a sixteen-acre park. Built in the 13th century and serving as the residence of the Dukes of Brunswick-Lüneburg for over three centuries, it houses Baroque state apartments adorned with Italian stuccowork and a Renaissance chapel. The Schlosstheater, founded in 1675, is considered Germany's oldest baroque theater still in operation. Queen Caroline Mathilde of Denmark was exiled here from 1772 until her death in 1775.

The Stadtkirche St. Marien ranks among the town's oldest buildings. Its tower offers views across the old town's rooftops and the castle. Access to the tower depends on opening hours posted on the facade.

Celle's synagogue, built in 1740, is the oldest synagogue in northern Germany. This baroque half-timbered building, located at Im Kreise 24, survived Kristallnacht in 1938 because authorities feared fire would spread to neighboring houses. Inside, a collection of objects that belonged to Holocaust survivors.

The French Garden (Französischer Garten) was designed in the 17th century by French landscape architects, though over time it has taken on a more English appearance. Linden tree avenue, flower beds, and a pond—freely accessible.

activities.text: Walk a loop along the Aller and the historic bridges—the best angle to see the castle reflected in the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Chemnitz was renamed Karl-Marx-Stadt in 1953 by the GDR and recovered its historic name in 1990 after reunification. The Karl Marx Monument, inaugurated in 1971, is a colossal 40-ton bust carved from Siberian granite, placed on a 13-meter pedestal. The locals call it the "Nischel," a local dialect term for "skull." In 2025, Chemnitz is European Capital of Culture under the slogan "C the Unseen"—150 projects and 1,000 events distributed throughout the city and 38 surrounding municipalities, in converted former industrial buildings.

The Kaßberg district is one of Germany's largest continuous Gründerzeit and Art Nouveau ensembles—a hundred blocks from the late 19th century with their sandstone facades and sculpted ornaments. It had largely escaped the 1945 bombings that devastated the center.

The Municipal Art Collections (Kunstsammlungen Chemnitz) present notably Germany's largest collection of works by Karl Schmidt-Rottluff—the Expressionist painter was a native of the city. The museum also includes an important collection of 19th-century paintings and contemporary art.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Arrive in Cochem via the road that follows the Moselle: steep vineyards frame the river's meanders, and the pointed silhouette of Reichsburg appears from afar, perched 100 meters above the village. Stroll along the Moselle quays—promenades lined with terraces, pleasure boats, and slate-roofed half-timbered houses.

The Marktplatz of Cochem and St. Martin's Church, built on the ruins of an 11th-century chapel, form the old town's heart. The square is framed by restored half-timbered houses and a former baroque town hall. From the square, stepped streets climb toward the castle.

Walk up via Schlossstrasse to Reichsburg (20 minutes from Marktplatz), the largest hillside castle on the Moselle. Built around the year 1000, first mentioned in 1051, it was destroyed in 1689 by troops of Louis XIV—then purchased in 1868 by Berlin merchant Louis Ravené, who had it completely rebuilt in the neo-Gothic romantic style then in vogue. The castle has 50 rooms, 6 of which are visitable by guided tour: the Gothic-style knights' hall, the Romanesque hall, the Renaissance dining room with its woodwork, and a room housing a 1 km secret passage to the Capuchin monastery in town. In the courtyard, a 50-meter-deep well once supplied water to all inhabitants.

Then ascend to Pinnerkreuz by chairlift (Sesselbahn), a viewpoint at 290 meters altitude dominating the Moselle loop. The panorama over terraced vineyards, river meanders, and wooded Eifel hills is the most complete in the entire Cochem region.

Burg Eltz is one of the rare European castles never destroyed and still owned by the same family for 33 generations—the first written trace dates to 1157, when Rudolph 'of Eltz Castle' was cited as witness in an act of Emperor Frederick Barbarossa. The castle rises from the depths of a forested valley, perched on a 70-meter rocky spur surrounded on three sides by the Elzbach stream. In 1268, three brothers divided the property according to a Ganerbschaft system (medieval co-ownership): three family branches cohabited in their own quarters around a narrow inner courtyard, hence the mixed styles—Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance—visible in the facades. The guided tour (~35 min) covers the knights' hall, the armory (with the world's oldest cannon arrows), the Rübenach Hall furnished in 1311—housing a Virgin and Child by Lucas Cranach the Elder—and the reception hall.

The Schatzkammer (Treasury), accessible with the same ticket, is the only place in the castle where photography is allowed. It houses over 500 pieces accumulated by the family over eight centuries: goldsmithery, jewelry, weapons, chalices, armor. It's one of Germany's most important private collections.

activities.text: One-hour panoramic cruise on the Moselle from Cochem marina. The circuit heads upriver toward the wine villages of Beilstein or Ediger-Eller, skirting steep terraced vineyards and medieval castle ruins.

Photo walk on the Moselle bridge in Cochem late afternoon: from the bridge, the view axis aligns quays, steeples, and castle silhouette—Cochem's classic composition, particularly flattering in golden evening light.

Approach hike from the village of Moselkern (on the Moselle bank) to Burg Eltz: a 2.5 km forest trail (35 minutes) along the Elzbach, progressively descending into the wooded valley. The castle appears suddenly at the turn of the last meander. This path is the best way to arrive—and avoid parking lot lines.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: Cologne Cathedral is Germany's most visited monument. Its construction began in 1248 at the initiative of the archbishop to house the Shrine of the Three Kings, brought from Milan in 1164 by Frederick Barbarossa—the golden reliquary is still visible in the choir. Work stopped in 1560 for lack of funds and only resumed in 1842 under the impulse of Romanticism and Prussian nationalism. The building was completed in 1880, after 632 years of construction. Its twin towers reach 157 meters and made the cathedral the world's tallest building from 1880 to 1884, until the inauguration of the Washington Monument. Its west façade, over 7,100 square meters, is the largest of any cathedral in the world. The 533 steps of the south tower lead to a panoramic platform over Cologne and the Rhine.

Cross the Hohenzollern Bridge to see the thousands of padlocks attached to its railings by passing couples—a tradition launched in the early 2000s that has become one of Cologne's most iconic images. The steel bridge, built between 1907 and 1911, carries both rail traffic and pedestrians and offers the most classic view of the cathedral from the left bank.

The Romano-Germanic Museum, right next to the cathedral, is built around a 2nd-century Dionysus mosaic discovered in situ in 1941 during construction of an air-raid shelter. Cologne—Colonia Claudia Ara Agrippinensium—was one of the most important cities of the Roman Empire north of the Alps, founded in 50 AD. The museum displays the vestiges of this Roman history.

activities.text: Walk through Cologne's old town—the Altstadt—between the cathedral and the 12 great Romanesque churches that punctuate the historic center. Cologne has the highest concentration of Romanesque churches in Germany: Groß Sankt Martin, Sankt Gereon with its 4th-century rotunda, Sankt Maria im Kapitol built on a Roman temple, and many others form a unique architectural journey.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Explore the mysterious Burg Berwartstein, a fascinating castle perched dramatically atop one of the rocky sandstone hills that characterize this border region. Once seized by notorious robber barons during the Middle Ages, this fortress is steeped in amazing myths and legends that the guided tours bring thrillingly to life. The hand-carved, candlelit tunnels beneath the castle create an atmospheric adventure unlike any other.

Discover the castle's remarkable architecture, including a small chapel adorned with medieval paintings that survived centuries of conflict and neglect. The robber baron history adds a thrilling dimension to the visit, with tales of ambushes, ransoms and dramatic escapes echoing through the stone corridors. This hidden gem remains surprisingly undiscovered by mass tourism, offering an authentic castle experience.

activities.text: Take the guided tour to hear the fascinating legends and explore the candle-lit underground passages

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: The Mathildenhöhe is Germany's most remarkable Art Nouveau site, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in July 2021. On this hill overlooking Darmstadt, Grand Duke Ernst Ludwig founded an artists' colony in 1899, bringing together architects, painters, and sculptors—including Joseph Maria Olbrich and Peter Behrens—under the motto: "Let my Hesse flourish, and with it art." Four international exhibitions (1901, 1904, 1908, 1914) produced an ensemble of 23 architectural elements: artists' houses, studios, the Swan Pavilion, a lily pond, and a grove of plane trees. These buildings became a laboratory of emerging modernism, inspired by both the British Arts & Crafts movement and the Vienna Secession.

The Hochzeitsturm (Wedding Tower), standing 48.5 meters tall, is the iconic building of the Mathildenhöhe. Olbrich designed it for the third exhibition in 1908 as a gift from the city to Grand Duke Ernst Ludwig for his remarriage to Princess Eleonore. Locals call it the "Five Finger Tower" because of its five ascending arches. Since 1993, an annex of the registry office has been housed here—weddings celebrated at the top grant access to the panoramic platform.

The Russian Chapel of Saint Mary Magdalene is integrated into the UNESCO site but predates the artists' colony. It was built between 1897 and 1899 by architect Louis Benois from Saint Petersburg, commissioned by Tsar Nicholas II—who had married Grand Duke Ernst Ludwig's sister Alix of Hesse (who became Empress Alexandra) in 1894. Its five golden domes stand out against the park's greenery and served as a place of worship during visits by the Russian imperial family.

The Hessisches Landesmuseum Darmstadt is one of Germany's most comprehensive encyclopedic museums. It brings together art (including the world's largest collection of works by Joseph Beuys), archaeology, geology, and natural history. One gallery is devoted to fossils from the Messel Pit.

The Messel Pit (Grube Messel), 9 km northeast of Darmstadt, is Germany's first natural site inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1995). This former oil shale quarry preserves the remains of over 40,000 specimens from a 47-million-year-old ecosystem—the Eocene epoch, when the first modern mammals were developing in a tropical forest. Discoveries include primitive horses the size of a dog (Eurohippus messelensis), primates, crocodiles, snakes, and insects with colors still visible. Access to the pit itself is by guided tour only. The finest specimens are displayed at the Senckenberg in Frankfurt and the Landesmuseum in Darmstadt. Fun fact: the pit nearly became a municipal landfill in the 1970s—strong citizen and scientific mobilization prevented it.

activities.text: Guided tour into the Messel Pit: the descent into the quarry allows you to observe the layers of oil shale in which fossils are still extracted. Working preparation replicas are visible. The tour lasts approximately two hours.

Explore Darmstadt's scientific quarter, nicknamed "Science City": the Technical University, GSI (heavy ion research center), ESA/ESOC (European Space Operations Centre), and EUMETSAT are all based here. The facade of the ESOC control center—from which European Space Agency satellites are operated—is visible from the street.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Immerse yourself in the Bauhaus universe in Dessau, where this revolutionary art school was founded in 1919 and relocated in 1925. The main building designed by Walter Gropius, with its iconic glass facade, embodies the founding principle of form follows function. Over 300 Bauhaus buildings dot the city, constituting the world's largest architectural ensemble of this movement.

Visit the Bauhaus Museum which houses the world's most important collection dedicated to this major 20th-century artistic movement. Works by legendary masters Walter Gropius, Marcel Breuer, and Wassily Kandinsky are displayed in a contemporary architectural setting. The Masters' Houses, residences of Bauhaus professors, offer a moving testimony to their avant-garde lifestyle.

Discover the rebuilt city center of Dessau, more than 85% destroyed during World War II. The Residential Palace, Town Hall, and mausoleum testify to the princely history of the town before the Bauhaus era. St. Peter and Paul Church and Georgium Palace, surrounded by its English-style park, complete this diverse architectural heritage.

activities.text: Visit Luisium Park, an 18th-century landscape garden with its small neoclassical palace

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Dinkelsbühl is often called Rothenburg's 'little sister'—but rightly less known, therefore less crowded. The town is surrounded by ramparts dating partly from 1370, with 16 towers and 4 gates still standing, accessible along their entire perimeter. Unlike Rothenburg, Dinkelsbühl suffered no damage during World War II—three-quarters of its houses are over 350 years old. Walk the outer rampart path (2.7 km, about 45 min) to see the towers, moats, and gardens bordering the walls.

St. George's Minster (Münster St. Georg) is Dinkelsbühl's central monument. Built between 1448 and 1499 in just 51 years, it's one of the most beautiful Gothic hall churches in southern Germany. The three naves, covered by a network vault, are of equal height, creating a rare sense of unified space in Gothic architecture. The Romanesque tower predates the nave: it dates from the 12th century.

The Marktplatz and Weinmarkt (wine market), two adjacent squares separated by the Minster, form the historic heart. The Deutsches Haus on Weinmarkt is a patrician half-timbered house in German Renaissance style, with a facade adorned with sculptures, one of the region's finest.

activities.text: Night watchman's round (Nachtwächter): every evening, a guide in medieval costume, lantern in hand, leads visitors through the illuminated old town to recount Dinkelsbühl's history.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: The Zwinger is Dresden's most iconic monument. Built from 1709 by Matthäus Daniel Pöppelmann for Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony and King of Poland, it began as an orangery and princely festival arena. Its official inauguration took place in 1719 for the marriage of Augustus's son to Archduchess Maria Josepha of Austria. Six pavilions linked by galleries enclose a vast interior courtyard with fountains and lawns—Augustus the Strong had himself depicted as Hercules above the Wallpavillon. The Kronentor, the gate crowned by a golden crown carried by four eagles, is the complex's most photographed image.

The Old Masters Picture Gallery, housed in the Semper Wing built between 1847 and 1855, contains over 700 paintings from the 15th to 18th centuries. Its absolute jewel is Raphael's Sistine Madonna, painted around 1512 and acquired by Augustus III in 1754 for the colossal sum of 25,000 ducats. Rubens, Vermeer, Rembrandt, Titian, Giorgione, and Botticelli are also represented. The paintings had been evacuated before the 1945 bombings—they were found hidden in underground mines and returned by the USSR in 1955 after ten years of detention in Moscow.

The Zwinger Porcelain Collection houses some 20,000 pieces of Chinese, Japanese, and Meissen porcelain. Augustus the Strong was so obsessed that in 1717 he traded a regiment of 600 Prussian soldiers for 151 Chinese porcelain vases—these pieces are still on display here. The collection also illustrates how the discovery of European porcelain at Meissen in 1708 arose from a royal commission for alchemy: Augustus wanted gold, his chemists invented porcelain.

The Semperoper, standing on Theaterplatz facing the Zwinger, is one of Europe's most renowned opera houses. The first building was designed by Gottfried Semper and inaugurated in 1841, then destroyed by fire in 1869. The second, also designed by Semper but completed by his son Manfred, reopened in 1878—Semper père was then banned from Saxony for participating in the 1849 insurrection. Razed by the February 13, 1945 bombings, it was rebuilt identically and reopened on February 13, 1985, exactly forty years after its destruction. Richard Strauss premiered several of his operas here, including Der Rosenkavalier in 1911.

The Residenzschloss, palace of the prince-electors and kings of Saxony, is one of Dresden's oldest buildings. Its construction dates to 1220; it was remodeled in Renaissance style between 1548 and 1556, then partially destroyed in 1945 and gradually rebuilt from 1986. It now houses several major museums. The Historic Green Vault (Historisches Grünes Gewölbe) presents 3,000 baroque goldsmith objects in their original 18th-century display cases—one of Europe's greatest treasure collections. On November 25, 2019, thieves broke a window at 5 a.m. and stole several diamond parures from the collection: the case caused a scandal throughout Germany.

The Fürstenzug stretches 102 meters along the outer wall of the Stallhof, the palace stable courtyard. This monumental mural depicts the procession of the 35 rulers of the House of Wettin, from Conrad I in 1127 to King George I in the early 20th century, accompanied by soldiers, artists, and scholars. Originally painted between 1871 and 1876 to celebrate 800 years of the dynasty, it was replaced between 1904 and 1907 by approximately 23,000 Meissen porcelain tiles to withstand the weather—it's the world's largest porcelain artwork. Despite the 1945 bombings, only 200 tiles had to be replaced during restoration.

The Frauenkirche, standing on the Neumarkt, is the symbol of Dresden's resurrection. Built between 1726 and 1743 by municipal architect Georg Bähr, its 96-meter stone dome—the largest north of the Alps—had withstood Prussian cannonballs in 1760 that bounced off it. It collapsed, however, on February 15, 1945, two days after the Allied bombings, from the accumulated heat in the sandstone. The ruins remained a memorial for 45 years under the GDR. Reconstruction, financed by donations from around the world, lasted from 1994 to 2005: 3,643 original stones blackened by fire were reintegrated into the new edifice, visible by their dark color among the light blocks. The golden cross at the summit was donated by British contributors, including the son of one of the pilots who had bombed the city.

The Neustadt, on the right bank of the Elbe, is Dresden's artistic quarter—it survived the 1945 bombings better than the Altstadt, and its Art Nouveau streets from the early 20th century have kept their character. The Kunsthofpassage, a series of five interconnected courtyards, is the best-known example of this creativity: each courtyard was entrusted to different artists. The most famous is the Court of Elements, whose blue facade is covered with gutters shaped like trumpets and funnels that transform rain into music.

The Brühlsche Terrasse, nicknamed "the balcony of Europe" by Goethe, is a 500-meter elevated promenade along the Elbe above the old ramparts. Developed in the 18th century on medieval fortifications by Count Brühl, it offers the classic view of the Altstadt silhouette with its domes and steeples. It's from this terrace that Romantic painters—notably Canaletto and Caspar David Friedrich—immortalized Dresden's panorama.

The Albertinum houses the Gallery of New Masters, covering painting and sculpture from the 19th century to the present. The collection includes works by Caspar David Friedrich, including Two Men Contemplating the Moon, as well as Impressionist and Expressionist paintings. The building itself, a 19th-century neo-Renaissance arsenal, was flooded during the 2002 Elbe floods—the works had to be urgently carried to upper floors to be saved.

activities.text: Walking or electric scooter ride along the Elbe to admire Dresden's reconstructed skyline
Observation platform at the Frauenkirche for views over the city

Ascent to the Frauenkirche observation gallery for a panoramic view of Dresden's rooftops, the Elbe, and surrounding hills. Access is via an interior staircase to the dome lantern, 67 meters above ground.
Walking or electric scooter ride along the Elbe to admire Dresden's reconstructed skyline
Observation platform at the Frauenkirche for views over the city

Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Düsseldorf's Altstadt is nicknamed "the longest bar in the world" due to its concentration of pubs and breweries in less than half a square kilometer. Beyond the festive reputation, the quarter preserves notable medieval remnants: St. Lambert's Basilica with its crooked spire (deformed by 14th-century drought according to legend), the 13th-century Schlossturm, the last tower of the princely castle destroyed by fire in 1872 and now a navigation museum, and the Rhine promenade laid out in 1993 when the former main road was buried in a tunnel.

The Königsallee, known as the "Kö," owes its name to an 1848 incident: King Frederick William IV, passing in a carriage on what was then called Kastanienallee, was pelted with horse manure by angry citizens. To smooth over the scandal, the city renamed the avenue "Königsallee" in 1851. Stretching 800 meters and bordered by a 30-meter-wide canal, it's one of Germany's widest avenues and has been a premier shopping street since the late 19th century.

The MedienHafen demonstrates that architectural conversion can be an attraction in itself. This former freight port decommissioned in the 1980s was converted starting in the 1990s with a simple rule: each new building had to dialogue with the harbor's history. Frank Gehry's Neuer Zollhof (1998) is the centerpiece—three buildings with undulating forms and distinct skins (white plaster, brushed steel, brick) that some interpret as a father-mother-child family. The neighboring Rheinturm rises to 240 meters and houses the world's largest decimal clock on its shaft.

activities.text: Stroll along the Rheinuferpromenade, 1.5 km of landscaped quays between Burgplatz and the MedienHafen. The promenade follows the Rhine with views of Oberkassel and its Art Nouveau façades on the opposite bank. In late afternoon, locals gather here for Altbier, the bitter brown beer typical of Düsseldorf.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Explore the German Wadden Sea National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site ranked alongside the Grand Canyon and Great Barrier Reef as a natural wonder of global significance. This unique tidal ecosystem stretches along Lower Saxony's North Sea coast, hosting millions of migratory birds and remarkable marine life. The dramatic interplay of ebb and flow creates ever-changing landscapes of mudflats, sandbanks, and salt marshes.

Discover the seven East Frisian Islands, one of Germany's most beautiful coastal treasures offering pristine beaches and unique island atmospheres. These car-free havens feature sandy shores, towering dunes, and endless skies where the rhythm of life follows the tides. Each island has its own distinct character, from bustling resorts to tranquil nature reserves.

activities.text: Embark on a guided mudflat walking tour across the exposed seabed during low tide, an experience unique to the Wadden Sea. Wade through this lunar-like landscape teeming with shellfish, crabs, and marine worms while expert guides reveal the secrets of this extraordinary ecosystem. The sensation of walking on the ocean floor as seabirds wheel overhead is truly unforgettable.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Wartburg Castle rises from a rocky spur 411 meters above Eisenach, deep in the Thuringian Forest. Founded around 1067 by Count Ludwig the Springer, its name springs from legend: upon first seeing the hill, the count allegedly shouted «Warte, Berg - du sollst mir eine Burg tragen!» («Wait, mountain - you shall bear my castle!»). The Romanesque palace (Palas), built between 1156 and 1162, is the oldest preserved residential building of its kind in Germany. The complex was extensively restored in the 19th century under architect Hugo von Ritgen at the behest of Grand Duke Karl Alexander - a restoration whose imagery directly inspired Ludwig II of Bavaria for Neuschwanstein. The castle has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1999. In 1817, student associations gathered here to demand a German constitution: the «Wartburgfest» is considered one of the founding acts of the German liberal-national movement.

In the Lutherstube, a small vaulted chamber in the Vogtei, Martin Luther lived from May 1521 to March 1522 under the alias Junker Jörg - «Knight George». Declared an outlaw after refusing to recant at the Diet of Worms, he had been «kidnapped» by fake brigands dispatched by Elector Frederick the Wise to protect him. In ten to eleven weeks, he translated the New Testament from ancient Greek into German - a version that did as much to standardize the German language as to spread the Reformation. Luther called Wartburg his «Patmos», referring to the Greek island where the apostle John wrote the Book of Revelation. The museum also preserves works by Lucas Cranach the Elder and objects related to Saint Elizabeth of Hungary, who lived here from 1211 to 1228.

activities.text: Panoramic view over the Thuringian Forest from Wartburg's ramparts. On clear days, the vista extends to the Rennsteig - the ridgeline that once separated Saxony from Franconia, and during the Cold War, the two Germanys.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Discover Emden, a historic seaport and sailing paradise on the North Sea coast. This maritime city at the mouth of the Ems River has been a center of seafaring for centuries, offering excellent conditions for sailing enthusiasts and water sports. The harbor atmosphere, maritime museums, and Dutch-influenced architecture reflect its rich trading heritage.

activities.text: Set sail from Emden's harbor to explore the stunning East Frisian coastline and nearby islands. The protected waters and steady North Sea breezes create ideal sailing conditions for both beginners and experienced sailors, with opportunities to spot seals and seabirds along the way.

Explore the unique East Frisian culture with its distinctive language, tea traditions, and windmill-dotted landscape. The flat polder lands stretching to the horizon offer superb cycling routes along historic dikes and through charming villages where time seems to stand still.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: St. Mary's Cathedral dominates Domplatz from a raised terrace, flanked by the collegiate Church of St. Severus - one of Central Europe's most impressive sacred ensembles. The current cathedral was begun around 1280 with a vast Gothic choir. Its glory is the Gloriosa: cast in 1497, it's the world's largest free-swinging medieval bell. It's rung only on grand occasions to protect the structure. The cathedral also houses the «Wolfram», a 12th-century bronze candelabra, and Gothic stained glass in the choir.

Fischmarkt is Erfurt's central square, surrounded by Renaissance merchants' houses, some dating from the 16th century. The neo-Gothic town hall, built 1870-1874, overlooks the square with its murals of local legends. Erfurt miraculously survived World War II bombing, explaining the density of its historic fabric - the city is often called «the preserved medieval metropolis».

The Krämerbrücke - «merchants' bridge» - is Central Europe's longest inhabited bridge: 120 meters long, with 32 half-timbered houses built directly on its structure. First mentioned in 1156, its current stone form dates from the 14th century. It recalls Florence's Ponte Vecchio or Venice's Rialto, except that its houses are inhabited and occupied by shops even today.

Erfurt's Old Synagogue, built around 1094, is Central Europe's best-preserved medieval synagogue. It kept its walls intact because after the pogrom of March 21, 1349 - when about 900 of the city's Jews were massacred - it was converted into a warehouse and fell into oblivion, protecting it during Kristallnacht. The synagogue-mikveh-stone house ensemble has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023. In the cellar, the Erfurt Treasure - discovered in 1998 in the wall of a neighboring cellar - has been permanently displayed since 2009: 3,141 silver coins, 14 ingots, and more than 700 Gothic goldsmith objects from the 14th century. The masterpiece is a gold ring adorned with a miniature temple bearing the Hebrew inscription «mazal tov». The treasure was exhibited at the Musée de Cluny in Paris and the Metropolitan Museum in New York before joining the permanent collection.

activities.text: Visit the medieval Jewish treasure of Erfurt in the Old Synagogue's cellar. Examining the Gothic goldsmithery - gem-studded brooches, gold rings, ceremonial vessels - one grasps what Erfurt's Jewish community was at its apex, before its annihilation in 1349.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Wander through Esslingen's remarkably preserved medieval old town, home to some of Germany's finest half-timbered buildings clustered in atmospheric lanes. The town center features extraordinarily large and beautifully decorated timber-frame houses that survived centuries of war and development pressures. This architectural treasure trove just southeast of Stuttgart rewards visitors who venture beyond the capital.

Admire the historic Altes Rathaus (Old Town Hall) and the twin-spired Stadtkirche that dominate the market square, both masterpieces of medieval civic architecture. The town's wine-growing tradition continues on the steep vineyard slopes that surround the old town, producing excellent local varieties. Canal waterways threading through the center add to Esslingen's picturesque charm.

Visit during Christmas market season to experience one of Germany's most magical medieval markets. The market transforms the old town into a fairy-tale setting with Middle Ages-era children's rides, torch-lit streets and the unique spectacle of betting on mice running into numbered wooden houses. Flaming braziers and costumed performers create an authentically medieval atmosphere unlike any other Christmas market.

activities.text: Climb to the castle ruins above town for panoramic views over the half-timbered rooftops and vineyards

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: The Römerberg has been Frankfurt's historic heart since the High Middle Ages. At the center of the square stands the Fountain of Justice (Gerechtigkeitsbrunnen) with its goddess Justitia, and on the west side, the Römer—a three-gabled town hall that's served as the seat of city administration since 1405. Inside, the Kaisersaal (Emperor's Hall) preserves portraits of all 52 emperors of the Holy Roman Empire, from Charlemagne to Francis II. This is where coronation banquets were held after ceremonies in the nearby cathedral. From the Golden Bull of 1356 until 1792, the Römer was the site of imperial elections. The hall and inner courtyard are freely accessible.

Continue to the Kaiserdom (St. Bartholomew's Cathedral), whose red Gothic tower rises 95 meters. Consecrated in 1239, it was officially designated the coronation site for Holy Roman Emperors in 1356—ten ceremonies took place here between 1562 and 1792. The current building is a postwar reconstruction, but the cathedral treasury preserves authentic pieces, including a Gothic priestly vestment dated to 1350. Climb the 324 steps of the tower for panoramic views over Frankfurt's rooftops.

Just steps away, the Paulskirche (St. Paul's Church) deserves a stop. In 1848, it served as the seat of Germany's first freely elected National Assembly—the founding event of modern German parliamentarism. It was one of the first buildings reconstructed after the war, a testament to its symbolic importance. The austere interior now hosts exhibitions and official ceremonies.

Cross the Eiserner Steg, an iron footbridge built in 1869 over the Main River, to reach Sachsenhausen on the south bank. From the bridge, the view aligns half-timbered houses of the Römerberg, Gothic towers, and the financial district skyscrapers in the background—capturing Frankfurt's paradox, nicknamed 'Mainhattan' for this coexistence of medieval and modern. Water level markers engraved in the pillars recall historic floods.

The Alt-Sachsenhausen quarter preserves its cobbled lanes and half-timbered houses. This is where the city's cider taverns concentrate: Apfelwein (or Ebbelwoi in local dialect) is served in blue stoneware pitchers in diamond-shaped glasses. Wander the alleys around Frankensteiner Platz before heading to the Museumsufer along the waterfront.

The Städel Museum, founded in 1815 by banker Johann Friedrich Städel as Germany's first civic museum foundation, is the crown jewel of the Museumsufer. Its collection spans 700 years of European art: 3,100 paintings, 660 sculptures, and over 100,000 drawings and prints. In 1937, the Nazis confiscated 77 paintings and 700 prints deemed 'degenerate'—the remaining works were evacuated to Bavaria to protect them from bombing. The museum preserves masterpieces by Dürer, Botticelli, Cranach, Rembrandt, Vermeer, Monet, and Picasso. The underground extension inaugurated in 2012, lit by circular skylights at surface level, houses contemporary art.

Head back toward the center to reach the Main Tower, one of the few Frankfurt skyscrapers with a public viewing platform on the 55th floor, at 200 meters height. On clear days, views extend to the Taunus mountains to the north and the Odenwald to the south. From here you best understand the 'Mainhattan' skyline: 39 glass and steel towers forming continental Europe's densest financial district.

The Palmengarten (botanical garden), open since 1871, is one of Germany's largest with 13 hectares of greenhouses and themed gardens. The tropical greenhouses shelter rare species from the Amazon, Africa, and Asia. It's a breathing space cherished by families and strollers.

Finish with a stroll along the Museumsufer, the museum boulevard that lines the south bank of the Main for about 2 km. Over thirty museums cluster here—cinema museum, architecture museum, Jewish museum, communications museum. Every last weekend of August, the Museumsufer Festival transforms this boulevard into one of Europe's largest outdoor cultural events.

activities.text: Walking tour through the Altstadt: circuit Römerberg → Kaiserdom → Paulskirche → Goethe House (Großer Hirschgraben 23). The writer's birthplace, destroyed during the war, has been completely reconstructed identically with original furniture and objects. You can see the study where Goethe wrote The Sorrows of Young Werther at age 23.

One-hour panoramic cruise on the Main departing from the Museumsufer embankments. The circuit passes the quay's 39 museums, under the Eiserner Steg, and offers a complete reading of the city from the river.

Apfelwein tasting in a traditional Sachsenhausen tavern. The local apple cider is served in stoneware pitchers in a glass called Geripptes (ribbed). The tradition of cider taverns in this quarter dates to the 18th century.

visits.text: The Freiburger Münster (Freiburg Cathedral) is a Gothic architectural masterpiece whose construction spanned from 1200 to 1513—one of the rare examples of a cathedral entirely completed in the Middle Ages. Its openwork tower of 116 meters was described in 1869 by Swiss art historian Jacob Burckhardt as 'the most beautiful tower on earth.' The interior houses original medieval stained glass commissioned by the city's guilds, an altarpiece by Hans Baldung Grien, and an Annunciation by Hans Holbein the Younger. The cathedral is the only religious building in Germany to house five organs. Each morning, the Münsterplatz market sets up directly at the foot of the facade.

The Bächle are small open-air channels that wind through the old town streets since Freiburg's founding in 1120. Their total network reaches 15.5 km, of which 6.4 km are underground. Originally dug for water supply and firefighting, they run between the sidewalks and give the old town a unique atmosphere. According to local tradition, accidentally falling into a Bächle means you'll marry a Freiburger. The old town also preserves its two original medieval gates: the Martinstor (early 13th century) and the Schwabentor.

Ascent to Schlossberg, a wooded hill east of the Altstadt, accessible on foot or by funicular. Once the site of a medieval fortress since demolished, it offers from its panoramic tower a 360-degree view over the old town, the Rhine plain, the Vosges, and the foothills of the Black Forest.

activities.text: Wine tasting of Breisgau wines at the Münsterplatz market or in an old town cellar. The Freiburg region enjoys Germany's sunniest microclimate and produces renowned Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir) and Gutedel.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: Drive the legendary Black Forest High Road (Schwarzwaldhochstraße) from Baden-Baden to Freudenstadt, one of Germany's most scenic mountain routes. This spectacular drive takes you along the peaks of the Black Forest, with incredible views plunging down into forested valleys at every bend. Numerous viewpoints and trailheads invite you to stop, breathe the pine-scented air and absorb the magnificent panoramas.

Pause at the Mummelsee, a mystical glacial lake surrounded by dark fir forests and wrapped in local legends about water spirits and lake nymphs. The viewing platform at Hornisgrinde, the highest peak in the Northern Black Forest, rewards visitors with 360-degree views stretching to the Vosges Mountains in France. Several hiking trails of varying difficulty branch off from the road through pristine forest landscapes.

Arrive in Freudenstadt, home to Germany's largest market square, a vast open space surrounded by arcaded houses that give the town a distinctly Mediterranean feel. This planned Renaissance town was founded in 1599 and rebuilt after World War II damage with remarkable attention to its historic character. The unique L-shaped Protestant church and covered shopping arcades make it a pleasant base for Black Forest exploration.

activities.text: Stop for short hikes along the route to experience the Black Forest's pristine nature up close

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Neuschwanstein Castle, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2025, is Germany's most visited castle with over one million entries annually. King Ludwig II of Bavaria, crowned in 1864 at age 18, commissioned its construction in 1869 on a rocky spur 200 meters high. He had 8 meters of rock dynamited to clear the foundations and, between 1879 and 1880, transported some 465 tons of Salzburg marble and 400,000 bricks. The construction site employed the most modern techniques of the era: central heating, running water on every floor, and a steel structure to support the throne hall. Ludwig II lived here only a few months in 1884 before being declared unfit to govern and dying mysteriously in Lake Starnberg on June 13, 1886. The castle was then unfinished—and never would be completed.

Walk up to the Marienbrücke, suspended 90 meters above the Pöllat Gorge and its 45-meter waterfall. This bridge, originally built in wood in the 1840s by Ludwig II's father as a birthday gift for Queen Marie, then reconstructed in iron in 1866, offers THE classic photo of Neuschwanstein: the castle stands out in its entirety against a backdrop of forests and Alpine peaks. This silhouette inspired Walt Disney for Sleeping Beauty's castle at the first Disneyland, opened in 1955 in California—it later became the Disney studios logo. The walk up from the Hohenschwangau parking lot takes about 40 minutes.

Hohenschwangau Castle, below Neuschwanstein, is the residence where Ludwig II spent his childhood. His father Maximilian II had it restored starting in 1832 in neo-Gothic style. The rooms, richly decorated with tapestries illustrating medieval legends—including that of Lohengrin, the swan knight—nourished the future king's imagination from his earliest years. Seeing Wagner's opera Lohengrin in 1861 at age 15 was Ludwig's revelation: the swan would become his emblem, omnipresent throughout Neuschwanstein. The castle overlooks the Alpsee, whose turquoise waters invite a walk on the trail circling it.

The Hohes Schloss (High Castle) of Füssen, summer residence of the prince-bishops of Augsburg since the late 15th century, is one of the largest and best-preserved Gothic castles in Swabia. Its trompe-l'oeil painted façades—false windows, false battlements, false oriels—are unique in Germany and date from the reign of Bishop Friedrich von Zollern (1486-1505). The Rittersaal (Knights' Hall) and its carved coffered ceiling merit the climb. The Bavarian State Gallery, installed in the former reception rooms, displays late Gothic paintings and sculptures from the 15th and 16th centuries.

Below the castle, St. Mang's Benedictine Monastery (Kloster St. Mang), founded in the 8th century and rebuilt in Baroque style between 1697 and 1726, houses one of Europe's finest collections of antique lutes and violins—Füssen is considered one of the cradles of European luthiery. The heritage museum also presents Jakob Hiebeler's "Dance of Death" (1602), Bavaria's oldest mural representation of a danse macabre. The monastery's splendid Baroque halls offer a glimpse of the institution's former wealth.

Wander through Füssen's pedestrianized old town along Reichenstraße, lined with painted-façade houses, which follows the route of the ancient Via Claudia Augusta, the Roman road connecting Italy to Germania. The luthier's fountain recalls the town's historic role in stringed instrument making. On Schrannenplatz, the former grain hall, historic pageants enliven the town in summer. The town, perched at 800 meters altitude at the foot of the Alps, has over 2,000 years of history—its colorful Baroque houses, alleys, and mountain views make it a spectacular terminus for the Romantic Road.

activities.text: Walk the circuit around the Alpsee, a 5-kilometer trail hugging turquoise waters framed by conifer forests and the rocky faces of the Alps. The panorama of the two royal castles from the south shore is spectacular. The trail is flat and accessible, perfect for unwinding after visiting the castles.

Walk down to the Lechfall, the Lech Falls, 10 minutes from Füssen's center. The Lech River, which originates in Austria, plunges here through a narrow gorge in a series of natural cascades. The bridge spanning the gorge offers spectacular views of the turquoise waters and erosion-sculpted rocks.

Embark on a cruise on the Forggensee, Bavaria's fifth-largest lake by surface area. Boats operate from early June to mid-October and offer panoramas of Neuschwanstein and Hohenschwangau castles, Füssen's old town perched on its hill, and the surrounding Alpine peaks. Evening cruises at sunset are particularly recommended.

visits.text: Neuschwanstein Castle rises on a rocky outcrop 200 meters above Hohenschwangau village. Ludwig II of Bavaria laid its first stone on September 5, 1869 as a "worthy temple" dedicated to his friend Richard Wagner - the interior frescoes illustrate the composer's operas directly. The king spent only 172 nights here before being declared "insane" by the government in June 1886, then found dead in Lake Starnberg three days later. The castle opened to the public seven weeks after his death and now welcomes over a million visitors annually. Book your guided tour slot well in advance.

Climb to Marienbrücke, a bridge spanning the Pöllat gorge, for the soaring bird's-eye view of Neuschwanstein - this is the angle that inspired Walt Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle at Disneyland in 1955, and later the studio logo itself. The bridge closes in winter due to ice.

Descend to Hohenschwangau Castle, Ludwig II's childhood residence, restored in neo-Gothic style by his father Maximilian II from 1833. The murals depict medieval legends of the Swan Knight and the Nibelungen - a backdrop that shaped the future king's imagination. Available as a combined ticket with Neuschwanstein.

Late afternoon, head to Füssen to see the Hohes Schloss, the former summer residence of the prince-bishops of Augsburg dominating the old town. Its exterior facade is adorned with trompe-l'oeil paintings imitating Gothic windows and carved balconies - an exceptional illusionist display. The castle now houses a Bavarian State Collections gallery.

Continue to the Lechfall, an artificial cascade built in the 18th century to prevent Füssen from flooding. The Lech River, with the turquoise waters characteristic of Alpine limestone torrents, plunges 12 meters in several tiers through a narrow gorge. From the König-Max-Steg bridge (1895), named after King Maximilian II, the view over the falls is striking.

activities.text: Walk around the Alpsee, the lake directly below both castles. A flat trail follows the shore for about 4 km. Ludwig II himself walked here regularly. In summer, rowboats are available for rent.
