visits.text: Gera is the birthplace of Otto Dix (1891-1969), one of the most important German painters of the 20th century. Born into a working-class family in the village of Untermhaus, he studied decorative arts in Dresden before leaving for war in 1914. His paintings of the Great War—The Trench, the War Cripples—are among the rawest and most disturbing visual testimonies of that conflict. In 1937, the Nazis confiscated 170 of his works as "degenerate art." The Otto Dix House and the Orangery of the Princes of Reuss, dedicated to him, house a selection of paintings and engravings including the famous Der Krieg (The War) series from 1924.

Gera's market square is one of Thuringia's most beautiful, with its Baroque town hall with mansard roof and entrance tower built after the 1686 fire. The neighboring Salvatorkirche is a Thuringian Baroque masterpiece with its frescoed ceiling and ornate galleries.

The Höhler are a network of vaulted cellars dug under the old town in the 17th and 18th centuries to store beer. Since the late 1980s, a section has been open as a museum. In October, the cellars host the Höhler Festival, one of Thuringia's largest folk festivals.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: The Georg-August University was founded in 1737 by George II, Elector of Hanover and King of Great Britain, to spread the ideals of the Enlightenment. Napoleon reportedly declared: "Göttingen belongs neither to a state nor to Germany—it is the university of Europe." Since its founding, 47 Nobel Prize laureates have studied or taught here, including Max Born, Werner Heisenberg, and Otto Hahn. One in four residents is a student. The Aula, the main classical-style building on Wilhelmsplatz, is the visible heart of the historic campus.

The Gänseliesel—Little Goose Girl Lison—is the Art Nouveau fountain installed in front of the Altes Rathaus (13th century). Every new doctoral graduate must climb the fountain, offer the statue a bouquet, and plant a kiss on her cheek. The tradition was officially forbidden but always tolerated. On some summer days, the scene repeats almost hourly.
The Old Botanical Garden, created in 1736, houses over 10,000 plant species and 19th-century historic greenhouses—one of Germany's oldest university botanical gardens, still active for research.

The Junkernschänke is a late Gothic 15th-century house with a richly carved facade, one of the most beautiful in the old town. The Stadtwall, a former medieval rampart transformed into a green promenade, circles the historic center.

activities.text: Walk a loop on the Stadtwall, the green ring that follows the route of the old medieval ramparts around Göttingen's center—about 4 km, flat and shaded path.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Görlitz is Germany's best-preserved city—more than 4,000 listed historic buildings in the center, covering five centuries of architecture from late Gothic to Art Nouveau, because the city emerged intact from World War II. Its prosperity in the 15th century rested on cloth manufacturing and trade in woad, a highly prized blue pigment. The Untermarkt, the lower square, is the heart of the old town: its colorful Renaissance and Baroque facades, wrought-iron balconies, and vaulted passages form one of Saxony's most beautiful ensembles. The city serves so often as a film location it's called "Görliwood"—Wes Anderson's The Grand Budapest Hotel, Inglourious Basterds, and The Reader were filmed here.

The Görlitzer Warenhaus, the former Art Nouveau department store from 1913 on Demianiplatz, is the building used as the hotel lobby in The Grand Budapest Hotel. Its large glass-roofed interior courtyard and multi-level shopping galleries are striking—the building was threatened with demolition before the filming saved it.

The Schönhof, dating from 1526, is one of Germany's oldest bourgeois Renaissance houses still preserved in their original state. It houses the Silesian Museum, which documents the history and art of the Silesia region shared between Germany, Poland, and the Czech Republic.

The Church of St. Peter and Paul stands directly on the banks of the Neisse, the border with Poland. Its Baroque "sun organ," built in 1703 by Eugenio Casparini, is one of Saxony's most remarkable instruments—its monumental case has remained intact since its creation.

activities.text: Cross the Old Town Bridge (Altstadtbrücke) to Zgorzelec, the Polish side. The pedestrian bridge rebuilt in 2004 bears a European Union plaque at its center. You can stroll for a few minutes on the Zgorzelec side—the two municipal councils have jointly managed many facilities since the symbolic reunification of the two banks.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Explore the Medieval Historic Town of Goslar, a UNESCO World Heritage Site that ranks among Germany's best-preserved medieval cities. Wander through cobblestone streets lined with over 1,500 half-timbered houses spanning five centuries, and discover the Imperial Palace where German emperors once held court. This architectural treasure trove offers an authentic glimpse into medieval Germanic life.

Descend into the legendary Rammelsberg Mine, a UNESCO World Heritage Site with over 1,000 years of continuous mining history. This extraordinary site, once a source of copper, zinc, silver, and lead, features fascinating underground galleries and innovative mining technology that shaped the region's prosperity. The museum showcases the remarkable engineering achievements of medieval miners.

activities.text: Discover the Upper Harz Water Management System, an ingenious UNESCO-listed network of dams, reservoirs, and channels built to power the historic mines. This 800-year-old hydraulic engineering marvel stretches across the Harz mountains, demonstrating how medieval miners harnessed water power with remarkable sophistication.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Step back in time at the Black Forest Open-Air Museum (Vogtsbauernhof), an extraordinary village of meticulously rescued historic farm buildings spanning 400 years of rural life. Each farmhouse, barn and mill has been carefully dismantled, transported and reconstructed on this picturesque hillside site, complete with authentic furnishings and equipment. This living museum offers an unparalleled window into the traditional way of life in the Black Forest region.

Watch craftspeople demonstrate traditional skills in the working wooden toyshop, where hand-carved Black Forest toys are made using centuries-old techniques. If you're lucky, the wood-fired bread ovens will be in operation, filling the air with the irresistible aroma of fresh-baked loaves. Farm animals roam the grounds, adding to the authentic atmosphere of a working agricultural community from centuries past.

Follow the excellent audio tour through the farmhouses to understand how families lived, worked and survived the harsh mountain winters. Interactive exhibits bring agricultural techniques and domestic life vividly to life, engaging visitors of all ages. The traditional Black Forest houses with their distinctive low-sweeping roofs represent architectural solutions to the region's heavy snowfall.

activities.text: Purchase authentic Black Forest handicrafts and traditional products directly from the museum workshops

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Hainich National Park is Germany's largest contiguous deciduous forest with its 13,000 hectares. Established on December 31, 1997, it is Thuringia's only national park. Its uniqueness stems from its military past: vast sectors were restricted zones during the Cold War, leaving them to develop freely for decades. The forest has thus returned to an almost primeval state, with old-growth trees, abundant deadwood, and more than 1,600 identified fungus species. The 1,573-hectare core zone has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2011 as part of the «Ancient and Primeval Beech Forests of Europe». A 9.5 km heritage trail traverses the core zone.

The Baumkronenpfad - canopy walkway - starts at the park center in Thiemsburg. Over 540 meters, the path gradually rises to 24 meters, at the level of the beech crowns, then climbs to an observation platform at 44 meters: Germany's highest platform of its kind, with unobstructed views across all of Hainich and the Thuringian Basin. The route is fully wheelchair-accessible thanks to an elevator. Panels explain at each level the life of the canopy and species diversity.

The national park center features an interactive exhibition on Hainich's limestone geology, forest fauna, and conservation challenges. A reconstructed «root cave» illustrates subterranean life. The center also provides information on the wildcat - an emblematic species visible at the dedicated village of Hütscheroda, a few kilometers away.

activities.text: Hike the heritage trail, a 9.5 km circuit through the primeval beech forest. The path follows deadwood zones, skirts forest pools, and crosses clearings where the black woodpecker - Europe's largest woodpecker - excavates its holes in old trunks.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Admire Halberstadt Cathedral, a Gothic masterpiece housing one of Europe's most important ecclesiastical treasures. This majestic cathedral, built on the model of French cathedrals, impresses with its harmonious proportions and medieval stained glass windows. St. Martin's Church completes this exceptional religious heritage with its preserved Romanesque architecture.

Explore the Klusfelsen near Halberstadt, impressive sandstone rock formations where medieval troglodyte chapels were carved. These sacred caves, sculpted into the rock by medieval hermits, create a mystical landscape unique in northern Germany. Hiking trails allow you to discover this site between nature and spirituality.

Visit the sandstone caves and Regenstein Castle near Blankenburg, a medieval fortress carved directly into the rock. This cliff-top citadel offers spectacular views over the Harz foothills and testifies to medieval defensive ingenuity. The cave dwellings of Langenstein, some inhabited until 1916, reveal an unusual way of life perpetuated for centuries.

activities.text: Hike to the Blankenburg sandstone caves and discover the rock castle of Regenstein

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Discover Halle-on-Saale, birthplace of the famous composer George Frideric Handel whose monument proudly adorns the market square. This cultural metropolis, the most populous in Saxony-Anhalt, houses the Martin Luther University of Halle-Wittenberg, one of Germany's oldest founded in 1502. The academic and artistic atmosphere permeates every street of this multifaceted city.

Contemplate Halle Cathedral and the Market Church of Our Lady with its four emblematic towers dominating the urban skyline. These late Gothic edifices testify to the religious and economic power of the town in the Middle Ages. Giebichenstein Castle, perched on a rocky promontory above the Saale, today houses a prestigious School of Fine Arts.

Marvel at the Nebra Sky Disc at the State Museum of Prehistory, the oldest concrete depiction of the sky ever discovered. This 3,600-year-old bronze disc, adorned with gold representing the sun, moon, and stars, revolutionized our understanding of Bronze Age astronomical knowledge. The Francke Foundations, a Baroque architectural complex dedicated to Pietist education, complete this exceptional heritage.

activities.text: Visit the Halloren Museum and taste the famous chocolate balls, specialty of Germany's oldest chocolate factory still in operation

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Hamburg's Rathaus, built between 1886 and 1897 by a consortium of architects led by Martin Haller, is one of Germany's most imposing neo-Renaissance buildings. Its central tower reaches 112 meters—exactly the same measurement as the width of the façade. The building rests on more than 4,000 oak piles driven into the marshy ground, and contains 647 richly decorated rooms. In the inner courtyard, the Hygieia fountain recalls the 1892 cholera epidemic that killed 8,500 Hamburg residents. Note on the façade: the 20 bronze statues of German emperors from Charlemagne to Francis II, and the golden phoenix that symbolizes the city's rebirth after the great fire of 1842. The current building cost twenty times the planned budget.

The Speicherstadt, the warehouse district built between 1883 and 1927 on former islands of the Elbe, has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2015. Its 15 large neo-Gothic red brick warehouses, set on oak pilings and connected by canals and bridges, form one of the world's largest coherent warehouse complexes, with 300,000 square meters. During its active period, the zone was a free port enclave: merchants could store coffee, tea, spices, cocoa, and oriental carpets there without paying customs duties. To build the warehouses, more than 25,000 Hamburg residents had to leave their homes. Today, the Speicherstadt houses museums, creative agencies, and restaurants.

From the Speicherstadt, climb to the Elbphilharmonie, the concert hall inaugurated in 2017 atop the former Kaispeicher B warehouse. The undulating glass structure designed by Swiss architects Herzog & de Meuron rests on the brick walls of the old warehouse—the rupture between the two structures is visible from outside. The Plaza, on the 8th floor, is freely accessible and offers 360° views of the port, the Speicherstadt, and the city.

activities.text: Walk through the MedienHafen, the former industrial harbor converted since the 1990s into a creative quarter. The conversion was conducted building by building, each architect required to engage with the harbor's history. The result is an open-air architecture museum: Frank Gehry's Neuer Zollhof (1998), with its three buildings featuring façades of white plaster, brushed steel, and red brick; Will Alsop's Colorium (2001), a 17-story tower clad in 2,200 colored glass panels; and Ingenhoven's Stadttor.

Harbor cruise: Hamburg has Europe's third-largest port, and boat tours from the Speicherstadt basin allow you to grasp the colossal dimensions of the container terminals and cruise ships. Departures are regular from the Landungsbrücken.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Follow in the footsteps of the legendary Pied Piper through the charming streets of Hamelin. This picturesque Weser Renaissance town brings the famous 13th-century legend to life with rat-themed decorations, colorful half-timbered buildings, and regular theatrical performances of the tale. The historic old town showcases some of Germany's finest Renaissance architecture.

activities.text: Enjoy the stunning views of the Weser Valley from the romantic ruins of Polle Castle, the legendary home of Cinderella according to German folklore. Perched above the Weser River, these atmospheric castle remains offer panoramic vistas across the rolling hills and forests of the Weserbergland, where fairy tales come alive.

Explore the Weser Upland hills, a landscape of gentle mountains, dense forests, and winding river valleys that inspired the Brothers Grimm. This scenic region offers excellent hiking trails through beech forests, past castle ruins, and along the picturesque Weser River with its traditional ferry crossings.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: The Fagus Factory is a shoe-last factory built starting in 1911 by Walter Gropius—at 27, his first independent commission—in collaboration with Adolf Meyer. Gropius eliminated load-bearing corner pillars and placed the structure inside the building, creating fully glazed corners—a radical innovation for 1911. This curtain wall principle in glass, which he would perfect at the Bauhaus in Dessau, was used here for the first time. The factory still operates today and continues to produce shoe lasts. UNESCO-listed since 2011—the only World Heritage site still in production.

Hameln is built along the Weser, with an old town whose half-timbered houses and 16th- and 17th-century sandstone facades illustrate the Weser Renaissance style. In the cobblestones, thousands of small bronze rat-shaped plaques guide visitors to the main sites—a nod to the legend whose earliest written traces date back to a chronicle from 1384.

The Rattenfängerhaus (Pied Piper House), built in 1602, bears on its facade an inscription recalling the event of June 26, 1284: "130 children born in Hamelin were led away by a piper dressed in many colors to Calvary near the Koppen and disappeared there." Some historians see in this legend an allegory of eastward colonization migrations (Ostsiedlung) in the 13th century—the rats, incidentally, only appear in 16th-century versions.

The Hochzeitshaus (Wedding House), built between 1610 and 1617, is the most imposing Renaissance building on the central square. Its mechanical carillon plays the Pied Piper melody three times daily with moving figures—crowds gather to watch.

St. Boniface's Minster (Münster St. Bonifatius) is Hameln's oldest religious building, its origins tracing back to a Benedictine monastery founded in 851. The current church preserves Romanesque and Gothic elements.

activities.text: Walk along the Weser from the historic center—the river borders the old town along its entire west side.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Discover Hanover, the vibrant regional capital of Lower Saxony, where modern urban life meets rich historical heritage. The city boasts magnificent royal gardens, world-class museums, and a dynamic cultural scene that hosts the world's largest shooting competition and the spectacular International Fireworks Competition. From the elegant Herrenhausen Gardens to the bustling city center, Hanover offers endless discoveries.

Visit the enchanting Marienburg Castle, a fairy-tale Gothic Revival palace rising majestically near Hanover. Built in the 19th century for Queen Marie of Hanover, this romantic castle features ornate towers, grand halls, and stunning views across the surrounding countryside. The castle museum showcases royal treasures and offers glimpses into aristocratic life.

activities.text: Stroll through the magnificent Herrenhausen Gardens, one of Europe's most impressive baroque garden ensembles. The Great Garden features geometric precision with fountains, sculptures, and the famous Grand Fountain reaching 80 meters high, while the surrounding botanical collections offer year-round floral displays.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Trek the famous Harz Witches' Trail through Germany's northernmost highland region, where stunning panoramic views reward every climb. This legendary trail winds through ancient forests steeped in folklore, offering spectacular vistas across valleys and peaks that have inspired myths for centuries. The Harz mountains combine natural beauty with mystical atmosphere like nowhere else in Germany.

activities.text: Experience the Harz National Park's 3,000 kilometers of trails perfect for mountain biking adventures through pristine wilderness. From gentle forest paths to challenging alpine routes, the varied terrain caters to all skill levels while showcasing the region's diverse landscapes of rocky outcrops, dense woodlands, and crystal-clear mountain streams.

In winter, glide through the white snowy countryside on cross-country skis across the Harz's well-maintained trails. The region transforms into a winter wonderland offering downhill skiing, snowshoeing, and toboggan runs amidst snow-dusted pine forests and frozen waterfalls.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Behold Burg Hohenzollern, one of Germany's most spectacular castles and ancestral seat of the Prussian royal family who later became German Emperors. This fairy-tale fortress, rebuilt in its current neo-Gothic splendor in 1850, crowns a dramatic conical peak rising 855 meters above the Swabian countryside. The castle rivals Bavaria's famous Neuschwanstein in beauty and surpasses it in authentic royal connection.

Tour the gloriously opulent interiors, where visitors don enormous felt slippers to protect the precious floors while children can dress in royal costumes for an immersive experience. The family still uses the castle for special events, so verify opening times before your visit to avoid disappointment. The treasury displays Prussian crown jewels and personal effects of Frederick the Great.

Hike to the neighboring Zeller Horn viewpoint for the iconic photograph of the castle floating above clouds that has made Burg Hohenzollern famous worldwide. The approach road itself offers increasingly dramatic views as the castle emerges from the forested hilltop. Hot air balloon rides over the castle at sunset provide an unforgettable perspective on this architectural marvel.

activities.text: Visit during Advent for the famous Christmas market held within the castle walls

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Marvel at Heidelberg Castle, one of Germany's most romantic ruins perched high above the red-roofed old town. This magnificent Renaissance fortress, partially destroyed by French troops in the 17th century, offers breathtaking views over the Neckar Valley and houses the world's largest wine barrel. The atmospheric ruins create an unforgettable silhouette against the sky that has inspired poets and artists for centuries.

Explore the Kurpfälzisches Museum on the pedestrianized main street, where fascinating exhibits reveal the Celtic and Roman civilizations that inhabited this region long before the castle was built. The museum houses remarkable archaeological treasures and artwork spanning millennia of human settlement in the Neckar Valley. This cultural institution provides essential context for understanding Heidelberg's deep historical roots.

Wander through Heidelberg's enchanting old town, a maze of cobblestone streets, baroque churches and historic university buildings that make it one of Germany's most picturesque cities. The famous Alte Brücke (Old Bridge) offers postcard-perfect views of the castle reflected in the river below. Stay overnight to experience the magical atmosphere after the day-trippers have departed and the city reveals its true romantic character.

activities.text: Join a guided castle tour to discover the fascinating stories behind these evocative ruins and explore hidden chambers

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: St. Michael's Church in Hildesheim was built between 1010 and 1022 by Bishop Bernward. Its symmetrical double-apse plan, characteristic of Ottonian Romanesque art, earned it the nickname "heavenly castle." The painted wooden ceiling of the central nave, created in the 13th century on 1,300 oak planks, depicts the Tree of Jesse and measures 27.6 × 8.7 meters—one of the extremely rare monumental paintings from the High Middle Ages to have survived, along with those in Zillis, Switzerland, and Dädesjö, Sweden. UNESCO-listed since 1985.

St. Mary's Cathedral (Hildesheimer Dom) dates back to 872. Its current form as a three-aisled basilica was completed in 1061. It houses the Bernward Doors, cast in bronze in 1015, and the Christ Column from 1020—the first major bronze castings since antiquity. In the cloister grows the thousand-year-old rose: planted according to tradition in the 9th century, destroyed during the 1945 bombings, it has since regrown from its roots. UNESCO-listed since 1985.

Hildesheim's Marktplatz presents a curiosity: its gabled houses were completely destroyed in 1945 and faithfully reconstructed between 1984 and 1989 from precise architectural records. Renaissance and Baroque facades that appear medieval but are less than forty years old.

The Roemer and Pelizaeus Museum houses one of Germany's most important Egyptian art collections, assembled in the 19th century by merchant Wilhelm Pelizaeus from Cairo. Funerary statues, masks, and papyri spanning over 3,000 years.

activities.text: Urban walk between Michaelisplatz and Domhof—the two UNESCO sites connected on foot in about twenty minutes through medieval alleyways.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Hohenschwangau Castle is Ludwig II of Bavaria's childhood castle. His father, King Maximilian II, had the dilapidated building restored from 1832 in neo-Gothic style, having medieval legend scenes depicted on the walls—including episodes of the swan knight Lohengrin, with whom Ludwig strongly identified. One room still displays the piano on which Richard Wagner played before the young king. It was here, facing the site of two former ruined castles on the opposite hill, that Ludwig II made the decision in 1868 to build Neuschwanstein there.

The Museum der Bayerischen Könige (Museum of Bavarian Kings), on the shore of the Alpsee, traces the Wittelsbach family's history from the Middle Ages to the 20th century. Collections include portraits, jewelry, and personal objects of Bavarian kings.

Walk along the Alpsee, a glacial lake with transparent waters at the foot of both castles. The complete circuit trail is about 3.5 km. In clear weather, the reflections of the Alps and castles in the water are striking.

activities.text: Late afternoon reconnaissance of the paths leading to Neuschwanstein and the Marienbrücke bridge. The ascent from the Ticket Center takes 30 to 40 minutes on foot. Identifying the route the day before allows you to depart at opening without fumbling.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

activities.text: Hike a section of the Hohenwarte Reservoir trail, a 74.4 km circuit that follows the reservoir's shores through the dense forest of the Thuringian Slate Mountains. The paths at times overlook the Saale's steep meanders from rocky cliffs, creating a landscape evoking Norwegian fjords - hence the local nickname «Thuringian Sea» for this region.

Walk in the Naturpark Thüringer Schiefergebirge/Obere Saale, which covers the Thuringian Slate Mountains and the upper Saale course. The nature park protects landscapes of heathlands, conifer forests, and deep valleys typical of the slate massif.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Schiller's Garden House in Jena is the modest dwelling the poet occupied from 1797, when he was teaching at the university. It's in this pastoral setting that he began his creative collaboration with Goethe - who had brought him to Jena - and started «Wallenstein». The original building is preserved with some period furniture.

The Zeiss Planetarium, inaugurated July 18, 1926, is the world's oldest continuously operating projection planetarium. It was designed by engineer Walther Bauersfeld of Carl Zeiss, who simultaneously developed the thin-shell concrete dome - an architectural invention that revolutionized 20th-century construction. The dome measures 25 meters in diameter. It's also Germany's largest planetarium. Current projections use a Zeiss Velvet full-dome laser system. The original idea dates to 1913, when the founder of the Deutsches Museum approached Zeiss for a «Himmelsschau» - a representation of the sky.

The JenTower is a 160-meter cylindrical tower built during the GDR era to serve as a Zeiss research center - hence its local nickname «Keksrolle» (cookie roll). At 128 meters high, the panoramic platform offers views over the Saale's steep valley and the surrounding limestone hills.

The Kunstsammlung Jena (Jena Art Collection) brings together more than 5,000 works ranging from the Middle Ages to the present, with a particular focus on German Expressionism. The museum is housed in several buildings in the historic city center.

activities.text: Hike a section of the SaaleHorizontale, a 91 km trail that follows the limestone ridges overlooking the Saale River around Jena. The cliffs plunge steeply to the river offering sweeping views over the valley and the city. Several trailheads accessible from downtown.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe is Europe's largest hillside park (240 hectares) and was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2013. Art historian Georg Dehio described it as "perhaps the most grandiose combination the Baroque ever dared between architecture and landscape." Construction of the park began in 1689 at the initiative of Landgrave Carl of Hesse-Kassel and continued for 150 years. Reservoirs and a hydro-pneumatic system feed a 350-meter Baroque cascade, a sound-effect grotto, rapids, and the Great Fountain—a 50-meter geyser, the world's tallest when created in 1767. The water features operate every Wednesday, Sunday, and public holiday from May to October.

The Hercules monument dominates the Bergpark from an elevation of 596 meters. The hammered copper statue, standing 8.30 meters tall, reproduces the 3rd-century Farnese Hercules. It's visible from across the city and serves as Kassel's symbol. Below the statue, a pyramidal octagon houses the head of the cascade. The entire ensemble—statue, octagon, and grotto—measures 71 meters in total height.

Kassel's documenta is the world's most important contemporary art exhibition. Held since 1955 every five years for 100 days, it takes place across multiple city venues—including the Fridericianum (1779, continental Europe's first public museum), the Documenta-Halle, and urban spaces. Permanent works from past exhibitions are visible throughout the city year-round.

The Grimmwelt, opened in 2015, is the world's largest exhibition space devoted to the Brothers Grimm's work. Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm worked in Kassel in the early 19th century, where they began collecting folk tales from the region. The annotated copies of Children's and Household Tales are inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register. The museum also presents their role as linguists and their contribution to the German dictionary.

activities.text: Walk through the iconic documenta sites in the city: the Fridericianum on Friedrichsplatz, the Documenta-Halle, and the in-situ works scattered throughout Kassel's public spaces. Even outside exhibition periods, permanent installations are visible.

Bergparkleuchten evening: several times a year, the Bergpark and its monuments are illuminated by colorful light installations. The water jets and cascades are lit up at nightfall, producing a particularly impressive spectacle on the Karlsberg slopes.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Kellerwald-Edersee National Park, created in 2004, covers 5,724 hectares in northern Hesse—the state's only national park. Part of its beech forests has been integrated since 2011 into the UNESCO site "Primeval Beech Forests of the Carpathians and Other Regions of Europe." These beech groves are among the best-preserved in Central Europe: some trees exceed 200 years of age and 30 meters in height. The forest is left to natural evolution without human intervention, creating "wilderness of tomorrow." Wildcats (which reappeared in 2007 after 60 years of absence), black storks, and 15 species of bats inhabit the forest.

The park center in Vöhl-Herzhausen offers exhibitions on beech forest ecology and the Eder Dam, which closes off the park's eastern boundary. The dam, 400 meters long, was built in 1914 and holds back the Edersee—Germany's second-largest reservoir at 27 km long. During World War II, it was targeted in May 1943 by Operation Chastise, one of the first uses of the "bouncing bomb" by the RAF. The breach caused catastrophic flooding downstream.

The Edersee's shores offer 50 km of waterfront to explore. The turquoise waters of the lake—fed by Kellerwald streams—are among the clearest in inland Germany. At low water levels, remnants of the village of Asel, submerged when the reservoir was filled in 1914, sometimes reemerge from the former shoreline.

activities.text: Hike through the UNESCO-listed beech forests on the Urwaldsteig Edersee, a 68 km trail that loops around the lake in 4 to 5 stages. The sections on the northern side, in the heart of the national park, are the wildest. The trail is perfectly marked with "U-E" in white on a blue background.
Boat excursion or water activities on the Edersee: sailing, windsurfing, kayaking, and swimming at developed beaches. Pleasure boats tour the lake from the Waldeck pier.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Climb the Herzogstand, a hiker's paradise culminating at 1,731 meters between two strikingly beautiful lakes. This iconic summit offers one of Bavaria's finest panoramas, embracing at a glance the steel-reflecting Kochelsee and the turquoise Walchensee. A cable car allows non-hikers easy access to this natural spectacle.

Discover the Walchensee, one of Germany's deepest and purest Alpine lakes. Its intensely blue waters, often compared to tropical lagoons, contrast magnificently with the surrounding snow-capped peaks. This natural dam lake served as the setting for numerous films and remains a well-kept secret among Bavarians.

Contemplate the Kochelsee, a more peaceful and accessible lake bordered by green meadows and traditional villages. It was here that painter Franz Marc, a major figure of German Expressionism, found inspiration for his famous works. The Franz Marc Museum in the nearby village pays tribute to this master of color.

activities.text: Panoramic ridge hike on the Herzogstand

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Königstein Fortress occupies 9.5 hectares on a sandstone plateau that dominates the Elbe by 240 meters—it's one of Europe's largest mountain fortresses. Never taken by assault, it was nicknamed the "Bastille of Saxony": a state prison from 1591 to 1922, it notably housed Johann Friedrich Böttger, inventor of European porcelain, who was imprisoned there to try to recreate his secret formula. During World War II, it served as an oflag for Allied officers—General Henri Giraud escaped on April 17, 1942 by descending a rope made of knotted sheets, an action that put Hitler in a black rage. The well dug between 1563 and 1569 to supply the garrison measures 152.5 meters deep—the deepest in Saxony.

activities.text: Walk around the fortress ramparts (1.7 km) with panoramic views of the Elbe valley, Saxon Switzerland National Park, and Bohemia. A panoramic elevator provides access to the plateau from below.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Explore Konstanz on the shores of the Bodensee (Lake Constance), Germany's largest lake straddling the border with Switzerland and Austria. The lovely lakeside promenade is lined with restaurants and cafes offering views across the shimmering waters to the Swiss Alps. The famous rotating Imperia statue at the harbour entrance completes one revolution every three minutes, a modern landmark with medieval satirical roots.

Visit the fascinating pile-dwelling open-air museum, a UNESCO World Heritage site that reconstructs Stone Age and Bronze Age settlements built on wooden stilts over the water. These remarkable reconstructions offer a glimpse into human life on Lake Constance over 4,000 years ago. Archaeological finds from the lake bed bring these ancient communities vividly to life through engaging exhibits.

Cross to Mainau Island, the famous 'Flower Island' where subtropical botanical gardens, a baroque palace and butterfly house create a Mediterranean paradise in the heart of Europe. The gardens burst with colour year-round, from spring tulips to summer roses and autumn dahlias. Nearby Meersburg Castle, Germany's oldest inhabited castle, perches dramatically above the lake with parts still occupied by the owning family.

activities.text: Take a boat cruise across the Bodensee to appreciate the stunning Alpine panorama from the water

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Stop in Wismar to discover the medieval old town classified by UNESCO.
Discovery of Kühlungsborn and its 4 km promenade along the Baltic Sea.

activities.text: Relaxation on the beach in a Strandkorb, typical seat of German seaside resorts.
Bike ride in the Kühlung forest and visit to the Buk lighthouse (1878).

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Lakes Route: Castles, Baltic Beaches and Hanseatic Cities

visits.text: The Thomaskirche is Leipzig's musical heart. Johann Sebastian Bach was cantor here from 1723 until his death in 1750—he composed the St. Matthew Passion, the Christmas Oratorio, and more than 150 cantatas here. His tomb stands before the altar. The Thomanerchor, founded in 1212, is one of the world's oldest choral ensembles and still performs motets every Friday and Saturday. Martin Luther himself preached from this pulpit.

The Gewandhaus Orchestra, founded in 1743 by Leipzig's merchant bourgeoisie—a clean break from the tradition of princely or ecclesiastical orchestras—is one of the world's oldest symphony ensembles. Felix Mendelssohn was its director and it was under his direction that Bach's St. Matthew Passion was rediscovered in 1829, after a century of oblivion. Richard Wagner is a native of Leipzig, Robert Schumann lived and composed here. More than 500 composers have reportedly resided in the city.

The Nikolaikirche, a Gothic church remodeled in the 16th century with a green and white palm-themed neoclassical interior, is the church of the Peaceful Revolution of 1989. It was here that the Monday demonstrations (Montagsdemonstrationen) began, with prayers for peace organized every week since 1982. On October 9, 1989, 70,000 people marched through Leipzig's streets—it was the turning point that precipitated the fall of the Berlin Wall.

The Völkerschlachtdenkmal, inaugurated in 1913 for the centenary of the Battle of Nations, is Germany's largest monument: 91 meters high, an interior staircase of 500 steps, colossal sandstone statues of warriors in the Wilhelmine monumental tradition. The October 1813 battle, which pitted Napoleon against the Prussian, Austrian, Russian, and Swedish coalitions, left 90,000 dead in four days at Leipzig's gates—one of the Napoleonic era's deadliest.

The Spinnerei, in the Plagwitz district west of the city, is a former cotton mill built in 1884 that employed up to 3,500 workers. Since the 1990s, its 10 hectares of industrial buildings have been converted into artist studios, galleries, cafes, and cultural spaces—more than 100 artists work there. The "Neue Leipziger Schule," a figurative painting movement influential in 2000s global contemporary art, was born here.

The Mädlerpassage, one of Leipzig's historic covered passages, houses Auerbachs Keller in its cellars—a tavern whose origins date back to 1525. Goethe frequented it during his Leipzig student years and made it the setting for a scene in Faust: Mephistopheles makes wine gush from a wooden table. Two groups of statues at the entrance depict the scene.

activities.text: BMW factory tour
Concert or motet at the Thomaskirche. Friday evening and Saturday afternoon motets with the Thomanerchor are free and open to all—it's one of Germany's most authentic musical experiences.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: The island of Lindau (68 hectares) concentrates the entire old town in the middle of the Obersee, the eastern part of Lake Constance, at the junction of Germany, Austria, and Switzerland. The harbor entrance is guarded by two emblematic monuments built in 1856: a 6-meter Bavarian lion in sandstone, sculpted by Johann von Halbig, and a 37-meter lighthouse—Bavaria's only lighthouse and Germany's southernmost. The panorama from the quays across to the Austrian and Swiss Alps is one of Germany's most unexpected. The name Lindau ('island where linden trees grow') is first mentioned in 882 in a document by a monk from St. Gall.

Maximilianstrasse, the island's main street, is lined with Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque townhouses with colorful facades. The Altes Rathaus (Old Town Hall) stands out with its entirely painted south facade depicting scenes from Lindau's history. Peterskirche is the oldest church on Lake Constance; it houses the Lindau Passion, a series of remarkable frescoes.

activities.text: Boat excursion on Lake Constance from Lindau's pier, toward the Austrian shores or to Bregenz. The lake spans 536 km²; on clear days, the Alpine peaks reflect in the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: Lorsch Abbey was founded in 764 during the reign of Pepin the Short, Charlemagne's father. It became one of the most important cultural centers of the early Middle Ages, housing one of Europe's largest libraries and a renowned scriptorium. Upon the death of Louis II the German in 876, the abbey became the necropolis of the Carolingian kings of East Francia. Of this once immense complex, only the Königshalle (Torhalle) remains standing: a monumental 17-meter portico built around the mid-9th century with a polychrome facade alternating light and dark bands over arcades inspired by antiquity. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1991, it's one of the rare Carolingian buildings to have retained its original appearance. Its exact function remains debated—library, reception hall, or ceremonial gateway? The Lorsch Pharmacopoeia, written around 800 in the abbey, has been inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register since 2013 as the first herbal medicine treatise.

activities.text: Walk through the Lorsch Abbey site: the footprints of disappeared buildings (basilica, cloister, monastic buildings) have been materialized in the lawns during the 2017 renovation by Topotek1. The medicinal herb garden behind the tithe barn is planted according to species mentioned in the Lorsch Pharmacopoeia.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Wander through Lüneburg, one of Germany's most charming Hanseatic cities built on a foundation of salt. The medieval salt trade brought immense wealth, evident in the ornate brick Gothic architecture, crooked historic buildings, and grand merchant houses. The leaning facades-caused by underground salt mining-give the town a uniquely quirky character found nowhere else.

activities.text: Discover the German Salt Museum housed in a historic saltworks, revealing how 'white gold' shaped Lüneburg's destiny for over 1,000 years. Interactive exhibits and preserved salt production facilities tell the story of the commodity that made this city one of the wealthiest in medieval Northern Europe.

Stroll through the atmospheric Am Sande square, surrounded by magnificent gabled houses showcasing the finest examples of North German brick architecture. The lively student atmosphere-Lüneburg is a university town-adds vibrant energy to the historic streets filled with cafes, boutiques, and cultural venues.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Immerse yourself in the history of the Protestant Reformation at Lutherstadt Wittenberg, cradle of a movement that transformed Europe. It was here, on October 31, 1517, that Martin Luther allegedly nailed his 95 theses to the door of the Castle Church, triggering a spiritual and intellectual revolution. This monumental portal, now in bronze, attracts visitors from around the world who come to pay tribute at this symbolic site.

Visit St. Mary's Church where Martin Luther preached regularly, a Gothic edifice at the heart of the old town listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The Castle Church houses the tombs of Luther and his collaborator Philipp Melanchthon. The market square, dominated by the Luther monument and Renaissance facades, forms one of the finest urban ensembles in central Germany.

Explore the many historic buildings of the old town bearing witness to this pivotal era in European history. Luther House, the former Augustinian monastery where the reformer lived, is today the world's largest museum of the Reformation. Crossing the Elbe bridge, you reach the floodplain meadows where pristine nature offers a peaceful contrast to this intense cultural heritage.

activities.text: Walk through the Elbe meadows to enjoy the preserved natural surroundings

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Discover the Green Citadel, an architectural masterpiece designed by Austrian artist Friedensreich Hundertwasser. This unique building with its organic shapes, vibrant colors, and green roofs represents a revolutionary vision of urban architecture. Its colorful columns and irregular windows create a fascinating visual experience that defies traditional architectural conventions.

Admire the majestic Magdeburg Cathedral, the first Gothic edifice erected on German soil. This imposing cathedral, whose construction began in 1209, houses the tomb of Emperor Otto I the Great. Its soaring vaults and medieval sculptures bear witness to eight centuries of spiritual and artistic history at the heart of Germany.

Explore the Elbauenpark and its Millennium Tower, an interactive attraction tracing 6,000 years of scientific and technological history. This 60-meter tower offers hands-on experiences across five levels to understand humanity's great discoveries. The park also houses the famous Sandman figure, an iconic character from East German television.

activities.text: Stroll through the Old Market and discover Otto von Guericke's Magdeburg hemispheres
Visit the Johanniskirche, a Gothic church rebuilt after World War II

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: The Albrechtsburg dominates Meissen from a rocky promontory above the Elbe. This late Gothic castle, begun in 1471 by architect Arnold of Westphalia, is considered one of Germany's first residential palaces—designed as a princely residence rather than a fortress. In 1710, Augustus the Strong installed Europe's very first porcelain manufactory here, under the direction of alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger, who had promised to make gold and discovered porcelain instead. The manufacturing secret was so well guarded that workers were not allowed to leave the castle. Production remained here until 1863, when it was transferred to new buildings below. The interior walls are covered with 19th-century frescoes illustrating Saxony's history.

The Cathedral of Saints John and Donatus, adjoining the Albrechtsburg on the same promontory, was built from 1250 on the ruins of a 10th-century Romanesque church. Its construction lasted until the end of the 15th century. It houses 14th-century Gothic sculptures depicting Emperor Otto I and his wife Adelaide of Burgundy, as well as paintings by Lucas Cranach the Elder. The Silbermann organ, installed in 1983, is one of Saxony's largest instruments.

The Meissen Porcelain Manufactory, in its current buildings at the foot of the hill, houses the most comprehensive museum on the history of European porcelain. The demonstration workshops allow you to see the different stages of production live—modeling, turning, glazing, hand painting—carried out according to methods unchanged since the 18th century. The crossed blue swords trademark, created in 1722, is one of the oldest commercial brands still in use in the world.

activities.text: Porcelain painting workshop at the Meissen Manufactory. The workshops offer discovery sessions where visitors paint a design on a porcelain piece themselves, supervised by a manufactory artist.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Monschau, nicknamed the "Pearl of the Eifel," is nestled at 517 meters altitude in the steep valley of the Rur. First mentioned in 1198, the town today preserves more than 330 listed buildings, including an ensemble of 17th- and 18th-century half-timbered fishermen's and craftsmen's houses stretching along both riverbanks. Its 13th-century medieval castle, built by the Counts of Limburg, overlooks the valley from a rocky spur. During the Battle of the Bulge in December 1944, Monschau marked the northernmost point of the German offensive front and was one of the few towns spared from destruction.

The Rotes Haus, the Red House, is an 18th-century patrician residence that tells the story of Monschau's golden age of cloth industry. In the 17th and 18th centuries, the town was one of Europe's great centers for fine cloth production—its woolen cloth was renowned throughout Europe. The house, converted into a museum, presents remarkably preserved period interiors with their Baroque and Rococo furnishings.

activities.text: Hike in the Rur valley around Monschau: several marked trails follow the river, cross meadows, and climb toward the forested hills of the Eifel. The Eifelsteig trail passes through Monschau and offers loops suitable for all levels. In spring, the valleys are covered with millions of wild daffodils, a spectacular natural phenomenon of the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Moritzburg Castle rises in the middle of a vast artificial pond, its silhouette with four round towers topped by copper roofs reflecting in the water—the image is striking, especially in misty weather. The first hunting lodge was built here between 1542 and 1546 by Duke Moritz of Saxony, whose name the castle bears. Augustus the Strong had it completely rebuilt in Baroque style from 1703, with the help of Pöppelmann, architect of the Zwinger. The interior of its more than 200 rooms is wallpapered with painted leather depicting hunting scenes—unique 18th-century pieces—and houses the world's largest collection of deer trophies. Napoleon stayed here in 1806, on the occasion of the coronation of his ally the King of Saxony. In winter, the castle hosts the Three Hazelnuts for Cinderella exhibition, named after the 1973 Czechoslovak fairy tale film that was shot here and remains cult throughout Central Europe.

The Fasanenschlösschen, the little pheasant palace, is located 2.5 km from the main castle at the end of a long perspective carved through the forest. This bright pink rococo pavilion, built between 1769 and 1782, was used as a secondary residence during hunting parties. Its miniature harbor with jetty and Saxony's only lighthouse—a pink lighthouse, too—served as scenery for the reconstructed naval battles that the princes organized on the ponds.

activities.text: Walk around the pond to see the castle from all angles. The promenade around the lake is about 3 km and takes 45 minutes. The most impressive view of the castle is from the opposite shore.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Neuschwanstein sits on a rocky spur at 970 meters altitude, dominating the valley by 200 meters. Ludwig II had it built from 1869 as a 'temple worthy of the divine friend'—meaning Richard Wagner, whose operas inspired all the interior frescoes: Tannhäuser in the singers' hall, Lohengrin in the bedrooms, Parsifal in the throne room. The castle was never completed. Ludwig II spent only 172 nights of his life there. In June 1886, doctors appointed by the government declared him insane and interned him; three days later, he was found dead in Lake Starnberg. The unfinished rooms were opened to visitors just seven weeks after his death—to repay his debts. Today, 1.4 million people visit the castle annually. Advance booking is absolutely essential.

The Marienbrücke bridge towers 92 meters above the bottom of the Pöllat gorge and spans a 45-meter waterfall. Originally built in wood then remade in iron in 1866, it bears the name of Marie of Prussia, Ludwig II's mother. From this bridge comes the world's most iconic view of the castle. In high season, lines form here—prefer early morning.

Arrive in Munich mid-afternoon. First discovery walk: Isartor → Viktualienmarkt → Marienplatz. The Viktualienmarkt is Munich's permanent open-air market, founded in 1807, selling Bavarian specialties, charcuterie, cheese, and beer at the central Biergarten. Marienplatz has been the city's heart since 1158: the Marian column (Mariensäule), erected in 1638 to celebrate the end of Swedish occupation, stands at the square's center.

The Munich Residence (Residenz) is Germany's largest urban palace—130 rooms, 10 interior courtyards, 40,000 m² of surface area. Seat of the Wittelsbach dynasty from 1508 to 1918, it offers a complete panorama of 16th to 19th century architecture and decorative arts. Its jewel is the Antiquarium, a Renaissance hall 69 meters long built between 1568 and 1571 by Duke Albrecht V to house his collection of ancient sculptures—it's the largest secular Renaissance hall north of the Alps. The barrel vault is pierced by 17 pairs of windows and covered with 102 lunette frescoes depicting Bavarian towns.

The Schatzkammer (Treasury), accessible with the same ticket, houses over 1,200 precious objects from Bavarian ruling dynasties: crowns, scepters, reliquaries, jewels, goldsmith's tableware—spanning two millennia of history. The Cuvilliés Theatre, designed between 1751 and 1755 by architect François de Cuvilliés, is one of Europe's finest rococo theaters. Mozart's opera Idomeneo premiered here in 1781.

Marienplatz and the Neues Rathaus: the Glockenspiel animates its 32 life-size figures at 11am, noon, and 5pm from March to October. The two scenes depicted—the tournament at William V's wedding in 1568 and the coopers' dance (Schäfflertanz) commemorating the end of a plague epidemic—have been performed since 1908.

The Frauenkirche (Cathedral of Our Lady), with its two 99-meter onion-domed towers, is Munich's symbol. Its Gothic construction dates from 1468 to 1488. Inside, look for the mysterious 'devil's footprint' (Teufelstritt) in the entrance floor—a footprint allegedly left by Satan himself, who came to inspect the building.

The Englischer Garten (English Garden), created in 1789, is one of the world's largest urban parks—larger than New York's Central Park with its 370 hectares. In the southern section, the Eisbachwelle is a permanent artificial wave where surfers practice in the heart of the city, an emblematic Munich image.

activities.text: Walk up from the Ticket Center to the castle (30-40 min) for the morning opening slot, before tour buses arrive. Visitor numbers exceed 6,000 people per day in summer—the earliest slots are significantly less crowded.

Walking tour through the Innenstadt: Odeonsplatz → Feldherrnhalle → Theatinerkirche (yellow baroque facade, built from 1663 as an ex-voto for the birth of an heir) → Hofgarten → return toward Marienplatz.

visits.text: Immerse yourself in the history of Nuremberg, Bavaria's second city and an imperial city of capital importance in the Middle Ages. This powerful city where the Diets of the Holy Roman Empire were held and where the imperial crown jewels were kept retains an authentic medieval atmosphere behind its imposing walls. Its Christmas market, the Christkindlesmarkt, is Germany's most famous.

Visit the Kaiserburg, the imperial fortress dominating the city from its rocky promontory. This complex castle, enlarged over the centuries, was the residence of all the emperors of the Holy Roman Empire. The view from its ramparts encompasses the red roofs of the old town, the church towers and the meanders of the Pegnitz River.

Discover the old town rebuilt after the destruction of 1945, where half-timbered houses stand alongside the Gothic churches of St. Sebaldus and St. Lawrence. The Nuremberg Trials courthouse, Albrecht Dürer's house and the Documentation Center on Nazism bear witness to both the dark and bright pages of this city of art and culture.

activities.text: Visit the Germanic National Museum, the largest museum of German art and culture
Stroll through the medieval lanes of the artisans' quarter

visits.text: Visit Linderhof Palace, the most intimate of Ludwig II's palaces and the only one he saw completed in his lifetime. This rococo gem nestled in an Alpine valley enchants with its hilly park dotted with gushing fountains, sculpted groves and exotic pavilions. The Venus Grotto, artificially lit, recreates a scene from the opera Tannhäuser with a navigable underground lake.

Discover Ettal Abbey, a masterpiece of Bavarian Baroque founded in 1330 by Emperor Louis IV. Its monumental dome, one of Germany's largest, houses celestial frescoes of breathtaking magnificence. The Benedictine monks still perpetuate the tradition of monastic brewing and liqueur distillation to this day.

Wander through the lanes of Oberammergau, a village world-famous for its facades painted with biblical frescoes and fairytale scenes. This tradition of Lüftlmalerei, trompe-l'oeil painting on plaster, transforms every house into a work of art. The village also hosts the famous Passion Play every ten years since 1634.

activities.text: Discover the local woodcarving craft tradition

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Experience Osnabrück, the historic university city where the Peace of Westphalia was signed in 1648, ending the Thirty Years' War. This cultural hub seamlessly blends medieval heritage with youthful energy, featuring impressive Gothic architecture, the magnificent Town Hall, and a vibrant pedestrian zone. The city's 1,200-year history unfolds through its stunning churches and well-preserved old town.

activities.text: Explore the surrounding Osnabrücker Land, ideal for mountain hiking with routes ranging from short day walks to multi-day treks. The rolling hills and ancient forests offer scenic trails through the Teutoburg Forest and peaceful countryside dotted with traditional farms and historic villages.

Cycle through the Braunschweig and Osnabrück regions on well-marked paths suitable for both racing and leisure cycling. The flat sections offer smooth riding through meadows and along rivers, while the surrounding hills provide challenging climbs rewarded with sweeping panoramic views.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Discover Passau, the Bavarian Venice, a spectacular city built at the confluence of three waterways: the blue Danube, the green Inn and the black Ilz. This unique geographical setting creates a fascinating chromatic spectacle where waters of different colors are slow to mix. The old town on its peninsula offers romantic perspectives at every turn of the lane.

Visit St. Stephen's Cathedral, an Italian Baroque masterpiece housing the world's largest cathedral organ with its 17,974 pipes. Daily organ concerts make this monumental instrument resonate beneath vaults adorned with gilded stucco and celestial frescoes. The exceptional acoustics of this sanctuary make it a high point of European sacred music.

Stroll through the old town with its typically Italian pastel facades, a legacy of master builders from the peninsula after the fire of 1662. The elegant squares, vaulted passages and picturesque staircases bear witness to Passau's historic role as a commercial crossroads between Central Europe and Italy.

activities.text: Walk along the quays at the confluence of the three rivers

visits.text: Pillnitz Castle is actually an ensemble of three palaces set in a 28-hectare park on the banks of the Elbe, 15 km southeast of Dresden. Augustus the Strong had received it from a disgraced former mistress—Countess of Cosel—and decided to make it a pleasure palace "in the Indian taste," meaning exotic. Architect Pöppelmann built between 1720 and 1724 the Wasserpalais and Bergpalais, two symmetrical buildings with characteristic pagoda roofs, framing a Baroque courtyard. A wide staircase descends from the Wasserpalais to the Elbe, where royal gondolas docked directly. In 1791, it was here that the Emperor of Austria and the King of Prussia signed the Declaration of Pillnitz, threatening military intervention in France if the French royal family were mistreated—which precipitated the declaration of war.

The New Palace, built between 1819 and 1826 after the 1818 fire that destroyed the Renaissance wing, closes the east side of the courtyard and now houses the Museum of Decorative Arts of the Dresden State Collections. Its classical dome is the only one of its kind in Dresden. The park also contains a Chinese pavilion from 1804 and a neoclassical English pavilion from 1780. The garden's camellia, imported from Japan around 1780 and planted in 1800, is Europe's oldest: it measures 8.9 meters tall and produces up to 35,000 carmine red flowers between February and April.

activities.text: Stroll in Pillnitz Park between the three palaces, French gardens, English garden, and greenhouses. You can also walk along the Elbe on the landscaped banks below the Wasserpalais.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Pirna is the gateway to Saxon Switzerland—and a medieval town worth a stop in itself. Its lanes around the Marktplatz have remained almost intact since the 16th century, with Renaissance facades with Gothic porticoes and houses with Baroque elements in adjacent streets (Schmiedergasse, Schössergasse). The painter Bernardo Bellotto, known as Canaletto, worked there in the 1750s and left several paintings of the town that show how little it has changed—some are visible at the Residenzschloss in Dresden.

The Marienkirche, whose steeples dominate the Marktplatz, is a Gothic church built from the 14th to 16th centuries. Its interior, modified in the Baroque era, retains Gothic ribbed vaults and a Renaissance altarpiece.

Sonnenstein Castle dominates the town from a rocky spur. Built from the 13th century for the Margraves of Meissen, it became a psychiatric hospital in 1811—the first establishment of this type in Saxony. Between 1940 and 1941, as part of the Nazi euthanasia program Aktion T4, more than 13,700 people with mental and physical disabilities were gassed in its cellars. A memorial on the site documents these crimes and honors the victims. The castle now houses the district administrative headquarters.

visits.text: Start your morning at the Partnachklamm, a 700-meter gorge carved up to 80 meters deep by the Partnach River through limestone cliffs south of Garmisch. A path hewn into the rock follows the glacial torrent - the water, flowing from Zugspitze glaciers, maintains an intense blue-green tint. Designated a natural monument in 1912, the gorge is a 25-minute walk from the Olympic stadium.

Continue to Lake Eibsee, its turquoise waters framed by limestone rocks and pine forests at the foot of the Zugspitze. From its shores, the cable car inaugurated in 2017 launches toward the summit holding three world records: tallest pylon tower (127 m), greatest elevation gain (1,945 m), and longest unsupported span in the world.

activities.text: Ascend the Zugspitze, Germany's highest peak at 2,962 meters on the Austrian border. The 90-year-old cogwheel train climbs from Garmisch to the glacier plateau at 2,600 meters, where a second cable car reaches the summit. On clear days, the panorama extends to the Dolomites and the Swiss Alps. Allow half a day and book ahead in season.

visits.text: Quedlinburg counts approximately 2,000 half-timbered houses spanning six centuries of construction—from the 14th to the 19th century. UNESCO-listed in 1994, the town owes this distinction to its imperial origins: first mentioned in 922 in a charter by Henry I the Fowler, it became the favorite residence of the first Kings of Germany. Between 922 and 1207, Germanic rulers stayed here 69 times.

The Collegiate Church of St. Servatius dominates the town from the Schlossberg. Built from 1070 to 1129 on 9th-century foundations, it is UNESCO-classified as a masterpiece of Romanesque art. In its crypt lie Henry I and his wife Mathilde—Germany's first rulers. Under the Third Reich, Heinrich Himmler transformed the crypt into a neo-pagan sanctuary dedicated to Henry I, whom he considered "the most German of German leaders."

The Lyonel Feininger Gallery presents works by the American-German master who joined the Bauhaus in 1919 and taught there until the Nazis forced its closure. He maintained a personal connection with Quedlinburg and deposited here a series of works that form this permanent collection.

The Marktplatz is framed by the town hall mentioned as early as 1310 and its Roland statue, a medieval symbol of municipal freedoms. The inner courtyards hidden behind the facades are worth exploring.

activities.text: Climb to the Schlossberg for views over Quedlinburg's rooftops and the Harz plain, then descend through cobbled alleyways. About 15 minutes from the market square.

visits.text: The Karl May Museum occupies two buildings: Villa Shatterhand, where the writer lived from 1895 until his death in 1912, and Villa Bärenfett, a log cabin in Far West style built next door. Karl May—who had never set foot in America when he wrote his novels about Winnetou—possessed here a collection of over 800 Native American ethnographic objects from the 18th and 19th centuries. His office, library, and Oriental salon are preserved identically in Villa Shatterhand. May is the most-read German novelist of all time, with 200 million copies sold worldwide.

The vineyards of Radebeul carpet the hillsides above the town, facing south on terraces supported by stone walls. This 507-hectare region is Germany's smallest and one of its northernmost—vines have been cultivated here since at least the 12th century. The dominant grape varieties are Riesling, Müller-Thurgau, and Pinot Blanc. Wackerbarth Castle, a state wine estate founded in 1728, is the valley's best known.

activities.text: Hiking in the Radebeul vineyards with views over the Elbe valley. Marked trails cross the vineyard terraces to the Bismarckturm at the hilltop. Allow about 2 hours for a complete circuit with views of Dresden in the distance.

Saxon wine tasting at a Radebeul winemaker. Local winemakers offer guided tastings in the cellar or on the terrace with views over the vines—Riesling, Pinot Blanc, and Goldriesling are the region's flagship wines.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Explore Regensburg, Bavaria's first capital, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This city with 2,000 years of history houses more than 1,500 buildings classified as historic monuments, constituting Germany's best-preserved medieval ensemble. The patrician towers competing in height bear witness to the power of medieval merchant families.

Cross the Stone Bridge, a masterpiece of 12th-century medieval engineering that served as the model for Prague's Charles Bridge. At 310 meters long and supported by 16 arches, this bridge offers a splendid panoramic view of the old town skyline dominated by the spires of the Gothic cathedral. It was once the only stone bridge over the Danube between Ulm and Vienna.

Admire St. Peter's Cathedral, the only example of pure French Gothic in Bavaria, whose medieval stained glass windows rank among Germany's finest. The old town abounds with architectural treasures: the old town hall where the Diet of the Holy Roman Empire sat, the colorful patrician houses and Renaissance interior courtyards.

activities.text: Walk along the Danube quays at sunset

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: The Bavarian Forest National Park (Bayerischer Wald) is Germany's oldest national park, created in 1970. It covers 24,000 hectares along the Czech border, continuous with Šumava National Park on the Bohemian side—together they form the largest protected forest area in Central Europe.

Start at the Tierfreigelände in Neuschönau, a 250-hectare natural enclosure where 45 animal species live in conditions close to their wild habitat: wolves, brown bears, lynx, European bison, otters, beavers, deer and large raptors. A 7-kilometer trail winds through the site—allow 3 to 4 hours. Bring binoculars, as some animals keep their distance. Entry is free.

Head to Rachelsee, a mountain lake at 1,071 meters altitude, surrounded by centuries-old beeches and spruces. Local legend attributes its name to the devil's grandmother, and tells that an evil woman was the last to navigate its waters, her coffin accompanied by a flight of black ravens. The trail from Spiegelau is well marked.

Stop at the Hans-Eisenmann-Haus visitor center in Neuschönau, which presents the park's history and wildlife. It's also the starting point for the Baumwipfelpfad, a 1,300-meter aerial walkway that winds through the forest canopy.

activities.text: Hike the marked trails of the park from Riedlhütte or Neuschönau. Over 500 kilometers of paths crisscross the park, some passing through "primeval forest" zones where no human intervention has been permitted for decades. The areas around Lusen and Rachel peaks are the wildest.

places.name: Rothenburg Ob Der Tauber

visits.text: Rothenburg ob der Tauber ranks among Europe's best-preserved medieval towns. The town, perched on a plateau above the Tauber River, has remained virtually unchanged since the Thirty Years' War (1618-1648), which bankrupted the city but paradoxically preserved it: too poor to modernize, it kept its medieval face intact. Its 13th and 14th-century ramparts still stand for 4 kilometers, punctuated by 42 towers and gates. The wall walk (Turmweg) is freely accessible and offers a walk of about 90 minutes at 6 meters height, with plunging views over the red-tiled roofs and the valley. After the March 31, 1945 bombings destroyed more than a third of the old town, international aid enabled faithful reconstruction—donors' names are engraved on the restored rampart stones.

The Plönlein—from the Latin planum, "little square"—is Rothenburg's most photographed corner and one of Germany's most reproduced images. At the intersection of two sloping streets, a slightly crooked yellow and red half-timbered house stands between the Siebers Tower (1385) on the left and the Kobolzeller Gate on the right, which descends toward the Tauber valley. This setting inspired the village in Disney's Pinocchio. Come early in the morning to avoid crowds and enjoy the low-angle light. From there, climb the Röder Tower or the town hall belfry for a 360° panorama of the town.

St. James's Church (St. Jakobskirche), built between 1311 and 1484, dominates the Marktplatz with its Gothic silhouette. It houses the Altarpiece of the Holy Blood, a masterpiece by sculptor Tilman Riemenschneider. The neighboring town hall, blending 13th-century Gothic elements with a Renaissance façade added in the 16th century, guards a mechanical secret: each day at set hours, the clock on the façade comes alive and an automaton depicts the legendary scene of the Meistertrunk—the "Master Draught"—where Mayor Nusch supposedly downed 3.25 liters of wine in one go to save the town from destruction in 1631.

The Burggarten, at the western tip of town, occupies the site of the Hohenstaufen castle built in 1142, from which King Conrad III ruled the Holy Roman Empire. Of the double fortress, only St. Blaise's Chapel remains, now converted into a memorial. This terraced garden offers Rothenburg's finest panorama: unobstructed views of the Tauber valley, vineyards, town rooftops, and the double row of ramparts.

The Medieval Crime Museum (Kriminalmuseum), housed in the former commandery of the Order of St. John, displays over a thousand years of legal history across four floors. Public humiliation masks, dunking cages, "gossips' violins" for punishing talkative women: these instruments reveal the brutal realities of medieval law enforcement. In complete contrast, the German Christmas Museum (Deutsches Weihnachtsmuseum) celebrates German holiday traditions year-round through thousands of decorations, nutcrackers, and historic Advent calendars—Rothenburg hosts one of Germany's oldest Christmas markets.

The Imperial City Museum (Reichsstadtmuseum), housed in the former Dominican convent founded in 1258, preserves an intact medieval monastic kitchen, Rothenburg's Stations of the Cross (1494)—a cycle of twelve panels painted by Martinus Schwarz—and Mayor Nusch's tankard, the famous 3.25-liter cup adorned with the emperor surrounded by the seven prince-electors (1616), which illustrates the Meistertrunk legend.

Fifty kilometers south of Rothenburg, Dinkelsbühl is the forgotten "little sister" of the Romantic Road—and that's precisely its charm. This former Free Imperial City, fortified by Emperor Henry V, has kept its ramparts intact thanks to an edict by King Ludwig I of Bavaria and was spared from bombings in both world wars. The walls feature about twenty towers, the oldest dating from 1370. The Wörnitztor, the town's oldest gate, opens onto streets lined with colorful gabled patrician houses, the Wine Market square (Weinmarkt), and St. George's Church, one of the finest Gothic hall churches in southern Germany, built between 1448 and 1499, with its net vaulting and St. Aurelian's reliquary.

activities.text: Join the Night Watchman (Nachtwächter) tour, departing each evening from the Marktplatz. In medieval costume, lantern in hand and halberd on shoulder, the watchman guides visitors through the old town's dark alleys, recounting Rothenburg's legends, scandals, and anecdotes—a tradition dating back to the Middle Ages when watchmen patrolled to prevent fires and disturbances.

Try a Schneeball (snowball), Rothenburg's specialty: puff pastry rolled into a ball, fried, and dusted with powdered sugar or coated in chocolate. This Franconian pastry comes in dozens of variations at bakeries along Schmiedgasse and Herrngasse.
