visits.text: Saalfeld's Feengrotten - «fairy grottoes» - are the galleries of a former alum mine operated from 1530. After closing in 1850, the mine was rediscovered in 1910, and the third chamber - the «Fairy Realm» - was discovered in 1913. Opening to the public occurred on May 31, 1914. In 1993, the Guinness Book of World Records designated them «the most colorful grottoes in the world»: the mineral formations - stalactites, stalagmites, concretions - display hues ranging from blue to red through yellow, due to the oxidation of iron phosphate (diadochite), a rare mineral. Since 1914, more than 20 million visitors have traversed the three chambers connected by galleries.

Since 1937, a gallery of Saalfeld's grottoes has housed Germany's first «Emanatorium» - a healing gallery by inhalation of underground air. The gallery's microclimate (constant temperature, mineral-charged air) was used to treat respiratory conditions. This therapeutic gallery is Germany's oldest.

The Grottoneum, an interactive museum adjacent to the grottoes, presents the site's geological and mining history along with exhibitions on the region's minerals and crystals. Children's activities are offered.

activities.text: Forest adventure trail near Saalfeld's Feengrotten, with family-friendly routes through the woods above the grottoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Savor Salzwedel, the town of Baumkuchen, where this traditional spit cake has been made according to ancestral recipes for centuries. This sweet specialty with its multiple golden layers, slowly cooked on a rotating cylinder, has become the culinary emblem of the Altmark. The old Hanseatic town with its half-timbered houses and Gothic brick churches provides a charming setting for this gourmet discovery.

Cruise on Lake Arendsee aboard the paddle steamer Queen Arendsee, which glides peacefully over the waters of this 5 km² natural lake. This nostalgic cruise evokes the atmosphere of old-time steamboats while offering enchanting panoramas of the wooded shores. The lake, the largest in the Altmark, is a popular destination for relaxation and water activities.

Discover the singular story of Gustav Nagel, legendary itinerant preacher and natural healer of the early 20th century. This eccentric figure lived in a cabin he had built near Lake Arendsee, traveling the region barefoot to spread his messages of peace and communion with nature. His legacy bears witness to the spiritual allure this peaceful region already held.

activities.text: Tasting of the famous Salzwedel Baumkuchen in a traditional pastry shop

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Jasmund National Park, established on September 12, 1990—one of the last acts of the East German government before reunification—is Germany's smallest national park at 3,003 hectares. It occupies the Jasmund peninsula in northeastern Rügen and is home to Germany's highest chalk cliffs: the Königsstuhl rises 118 meters above the Baltic. The chalk that forms these cliffs was deposited about 70 million years ago. Every storm carries off entire sections of cliff into the sea, exposing fossils of sea urchins and sponges. Painter Caspar David Friedrich immortalized these cliffs in 1818 in his painting Chalk Cliffs on Rügen, which made them a symbol of German Romanticism.

The Hochuferweg, a trail that follows the cliff between Sassnitz and Lohme for several kilometers, is one of the most spectacular on the Baltic coast. It passes through the Stubnitz beech forest—493 hectares of old-growth beech woods listed as a UNESCO World Natural Heritage site—and offers overhanging views of the sea. At the Königsstuhl, since 2023, an elliptical 185-meter walkway floats above the cliff from a cable-stayed mast, offering a vertiginous perspective on the chalk white and Baltic blue.

activities.text: Hike from Sassnitz to the Königsstuhl along the coastal Hochuferweg: about 10 km round trip through the beech forest and cliff viewpoints. The trail alternates between deep forest passages and windows open to the sea. Allow 3 to 4 hours. The park is also accessible by bus from Sassnitz to the Königsstuhl visitor center.

The harbor of Sassnitz, former ferry port to Sweden and Poland, now hosts fishing and pleasure boats. Walk along the quays in late afternoon to watch fishermen return and the fish halls. Several terraces serve freshly smoked herring, a local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Board a ferry from Prien-Stock harbor to reach Herreninsel Island on Chiemsee, Bavaria's largest lake. The crossing takes about 20 minutes. In 1873, Ludwig II purchased the entire island for 350,000 florins from an industrialist who planned to clear-cut all its forests. Construction of the palace began in 1878 under architect Georg von Dollmann.

Tour Herrenchiemsee Palace, Ludwig II's final and most lavish project - and where he spent the least time: just 16 days total. The king spent more money on this single construction than on Neuschwanstein and Linderhof combined. At his death in 1886, 50 rooms remained unfinished. The Great Hall of Mirrors, inspired by Versailles, measures 98 meters - 25 meters longer than its French model. To illuminate it, 70 servants had to light 33 candelabras and 52 chandeliers.

After the palace, take the boat to Fraueninsel (Women's Island), where the Frauenwörth Benedictine convent has existed for 1,200 years - one of Germany's oldest monastic communities still in operation. Walk around the small island (one hour on foot) among fishermen's houses and monastery gardens. The nuns produce a renowned local brandy.

Board one of the electric boats on Königssee from Schönau - the only craft allowed on this protected lake since 1978 within Berchtesgaden National Park. The lake, 8 km long and 190 meters deep, lies trapped between vertical limestone walls. Midway across, the pilot plays trumpet against the rock face to demonstrate the extraordinary echo.

Disembark at St. Bartholomä, a peninsula accessible only by boat. The baroque pilgrimage church, first built in 1134 and remodeled in the early 18th century, is recognizable from afar by its red onion domes. The ensemble of church and former royal hunting lodge, silhouetted against the 1,800-meter vertical east face of the Watzmann, is one of Germany's most photographed scenes.

Continue by boat to Salet to walk to the Obersee - a flat 15-minute walk from the dock. This smaller lake, with no boats whatsoever, displays absolutely transparent water and perfect reflections of the surrounding rock walls. Since commercial fishing ended in 1978, the lake has returned to wilderness.

Ride the Jennerbahn to reach the Jenner summit at 1,800 meters. The view embraces the entire Königssee, the east face of the Watzmann, and the Berchtesgaden Alps on the Austrian side. Salzburg is 30 km away - this is where the circuit naturally ends for those returning rental cars in Austria.

activities.text: Late afternoon from Schönau am Königssee, stroll the Chiemsee shores (Priener Uferweg) or follow the lakeside paths from Prien. Sunset with the Alps as backdrop.

Hike the Malerwinkel-Rundweg, a panoramic forest trail above the Königssee offering the classic elevated view of the lake. In 2015, this spot ranked as Germany's 12th most popular tourist attraction. Allow 1 to 2 hours depending on your pace.

visits.text: Explore Schönebeck, the salt town, whose millennial history is intimately linked to the extraction of white gold. The Salt Museum (Salzlandmuseum) takes you on a fascinating journey through the centuries to understand the economic and social importance of this precious resource. The picturesque old town with its colorful half-timbered houses invites leisurely strolls through streets steeped in history.

Relax in the spa district of Bad Salzelmen, where the majestic Gradierwerk (graduation tower) diffuses therapeutic salt spray into the air. This monumental wooden structure, where salt water trickles over blackthorn bundles, creates a unique iodine-rich microclimate. The salt bath offers an authentic wellness experience inherited from German spa traditions.

Admire Europe's longest inhabited half-timbered house on the Elbe, a spectacular testimony to traditional German architecture. The Salt Flower, a contemporary art installation on the river, dialogues with this historical heritage. St. Johannis Church completes this architectural panorama with its tower dominating the town.

activities.text: Visit the Pömmelte sanctuary, a cultic site over 4,000 years old with its reconstructed circular ditch

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Schotten is the gateway to the Naturpark Vulkanregion Vogelsberg, Germany's oldest nature park. The Vogelsberg is Europe's largest contiguous volcanic massif: it formed approximately 20 million years ago during a period of intense Tertiary volcanic activity. Basaltic lavas created a topography of rounded domes, peat bogs, and springs. The Vulkaneum in Schotten, a museum-interpretation center renovated in 2018, presents the geology and ecology of the massif with interactive stations.

The Vogelsberg landscapes are characterized by their mixed beech and spruce forests, flowering meadows, and dry-stone hedges. The central plateau—the Oberwald—is covered with forests traversed by springs and small streams. The slopes are dotted with basaltic formations with distinctive silhouettes. The region forms a water recharge zone for much of Hesse.

The Hoherodskopf (764 m) is the most iconic accessible summit in the Vogelsberg alongside the Taufstein (773 m). The Naturpark information center is located here, with a permanent exhibition on volcanic geology and the massif's biodiversity. In winter, cross-country ski trails are groomed on the plateau. In summer, a 7 km trail loops around the summit, passing through peat bog, streams, and several viewpoints.

activities.text: Hike a section of the Vulkanring Vogelsberg, a circular long-distance trail that crosses the massif along its most remarkable geological formations: basalt flows, volcanic columns, and "Bilstein" cliffs. Nature guides offer guided excursions from Hoherodskopf.
Cycling or e-biking in the Vogelsberg on the marked trail network. Elevation changes remain moderate on the plateau. Bicycles can be rented in several park villages.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Discover Schwäbisch Hall, a beautiful and charming town that was once a major center of salt production, the precious 'white gold' that created its wealth. The old town center winds through streets of magnificent half-timbered buildings to a gorgeous central market square dominated by the imposing St. Michael's Church. The covered wooden bridge spanning the Kocher River creates one of Germany's most photographed scenes.

Visit the local museum to learn about the fascinating history of salt making that gave the town its name and fortune. Salt has been extracted here since Celtic times, and the industry shaped every aspect of local life, architecture and culture. The Hällisch-Fränkisches Museum brings this heritage to life through engaging exhibits about the salt workers and merchants who built the town.

Explore the Großcomburg monastery complex perched on a hill just above the town, where 16th-century defensive walls still stand protecting Romanesque chapel and cloister within. The monastery's peaceful courtyards and historic architecture offer a contemplative contrast to the bustling town below. Check the city's festival calendar, as Schwäbisch Hall hosts excellent cultural events throughout the year.

activities.text: Relax in the riverside park along the Kocher and admire views of the picturesque covered bridge

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Visit of Schwerin castle, a fairy-tale palace located on an island in the middle of the lake.
Discovery of the historic city center and the Gothic brick cathedral of Schwerin.

activities.text: Walk along the shores of Lake Schwerin and exploration of the parliamentary district.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Lakes Route: Castles, Baltic Beaches and Hanseatic Cities

visits.text: Lose yourself in the magnificent gardens of Schloss Schwetzingen, a stunning summer palace with acres of meticulously landscaped grounds that rank among Germany's finest baroque gardens. The salmon-pink palace provides an elegant backdrop to formal parterres, romantic temples and winding woodland paths designed to delight at every turn. This princely retreat was created for the Electors Palatine as an escape from court life in Heidelberg.

Discover the garden's extraordinary follies, including a mosque, Roman temple and bath house that reflect 18th-century fascination with exotic cultures. The circular parterre and grand water features demonstrate the height of baroque garden design and horticultural artistry. If visiting in May, the town's famous white asparagus festival celebrates this prized local delicacy with culinary events throughout the region.

activities.text: Enjoy lunch at one of the charming restaurants along the road leading to the palace gardens

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Seiffen is the iconic village of the Ore Mountains (Erzgebirge), nestled at 700 meters altitude on the Czech border. When the region's mines were exhausted in the 18th century, inhabitants converted to woodworking. In the 19th century, the village established itself as the world center for miniature toys and Christmas decorations. There are more than a hundred craftsmen and forty shops. The wood-turning technique called "Reifendrehen"—a log slice is turned then cut like a pie to obtain dozens of identical animal silhouettes—is unique to Seiffen and inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list.

The Seiffen Toy Museum traces the village's history and its craftsmanship since the 18th century. Christmas pyramids—those wooden candle carousels that rotate by thermal convection—are exhibited in period versions. The nutcrackers in Saxon soldier form, whose first version was created in the region to ridicule authority figures, are the other local specialty. The tradition of the mining angel and the Schwibbogen, the luminous wooden arch placed in windows, also finds its explanation here: these objects recall the candles that miners placed at the mine entrance on Christmas Eve.

activities.text: Hiking in the Ore Mountains from the village. Marked trails cross conifer forests and ridges with views over the Czech border. In winter, the cross-country ski trail network is one of Germany's densest.

Historic steam train ride on the Fichtelbergbahn network, the narrow-gauge line connecting Cranzahl to Oberwiesenthal since 1897. Period steam locomotives cross the villages and forests of the Ore Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Leuchtenburg dominates the Saale Valley from 395 meters altitude, nicknamed «the Queen of the Saale Valley». Its first documented mention dates to 1221, when it was a military base of the Lords of Lobdeburg. It changed hands frequently and became in 1396 the administrative center of the Wettin villages. In 1553, an 80-meter-deep well was dug into the rock - Thuringia's second-deepest at the time. After its administration closed in 1705, it served variously as a prison, hospice, and asylum. Between 1920 and 1997, Thuringia's first youth hostel operated there. Purchased at auction in 2007 by the Leuchtenburg Foundation, the castle was restored and a new permanent exhibition opened in 2014.

The Porzellanwelten Leuchtenburg traces 800 years of Thuringian porcelain history in the castle's halls. The exhibition explores the art, technique, and culture of this craft of which the town of Kahla, at the castle's foot, is a historic center. Modern installations deliberately contrast with the medieval walls.

The Mythos Burg exhibition presents the castle's medieval history and legends linked to Leuchtenburg. The keep, accessible by a steep staircase, offers a 360-degree panorama over the Harz, the Thuringian Forest, and the Saale's steep valley.

activities.text: Panoramic view from Leuchtenburg's keep: on clear days, the vista extends to the Harz in the north and the Thuringian Forest in the south. The ascent is via a path or stairs from parking areas below.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Experience the breathtaking Lüneburg Heath, one of Germany's most enchanting natural landscapes. This vast expanse of purple heather moorland, dotted with ancient juniper bushes and grazing Heidschnucken sheep, creates a magical atmosphere especially during the August-September flowering season. The heath's peaceful beauty has inspired poets and painters for centuries.

Get your adrenaline pumping at Heide-Park Soltau, home to the largest wooden roller coaster in the world. This premier theme park combines heart-stopping rides with themed lands, live entertainment, and family attractions spread across a beautifully landscaped setting. Thrill-seekers and families alike find endless excitement in Germany's second-largest amusement park.

activities.text: Marvel at exotic species from around the globe at Walsrode Bird Park, one of the world's largest bird parks. Over 4,000 birds from 650 species inhabit this spectacular 24-hectare garden featuring stunning aviaries, tropical houses, and interactive feeding experiences. From tiny hummingbirds to majestic eagles, the diversity of birdlife creates a colorful paradise.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Lichtenstein Castle stands on a rocky spur at 817 meters altitude, above the Echaz valley in the Swabian Jura. In 1826, writer Wilhelm Hauff published a historical novel featuring the medieval castle of Lichtenstein as its setting. The novel was a resounding success, and Count Wilhelm von Urach, cousin of the King of Württemberg, was so enthused he purchased the site and had the current neo-Gothic castle built between 1840 and 1842—a few years before Ludwig II of Bavaria did the same at Neuschwanstein. The interior houses a collection of medieval weapons and wall frescoes. From the ramparts, the view over the valley and Swabian Jura plateaus is remarkable.

Hohenzollern Castle perches at 855 meters atop Mount Zoller, an isolated outlying hill above the Swabian plain. It's the ancestral seat of the Hohenzollern family, from which Prussia's kings and Germany's emperors descended. The first fortress was erected in the 11th century; destroyed in 1423 after a ten-month siege, rebuilt from 1454 to 1461. The current castle, third version, was commissioned in 1846 by King Frederick William IV of Prussia and designed by architect Friedrich August Stüler in neo-Gothic style. Among the collection's pieces: the Prussian royal crown, a letter from President George Washington thanking Baron von Steuben for his role in the American War of Independence, and personal effects of Frederick the Great. On clear days, views from the ramparts extend to the Black Forest.

activities.text: Sunset viewpoint from the overlooks below Hohenzollern Castle parking lot. The fortress silhouette cutting against the sunset over the Swabian hills is one of Baden-Württemberg's most photographed panoramas.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: Stand in awe before the Speyer Cathedral (Speyer Dom), a monumental Romanesque masterpiece that served as the principal church of the Holy Roman Empire. This UNESCO World Heritage site, one of the largest Romanesque churches in the world, houses the crypts of eight German emperors and kings beneath its soaring vaulted ceilings. The cathedral's twin towers and red sandstone facade have dominated the Rhine plain for nearly a thousand years.

Descend into the imperial crypt to pay respects at the final resting place of medieval rulers who once shaped the destiny of Europe. The atmospheric underground chambers feature simple yet powerful stone sarcophagi that connect visitors directly to the empire's medieval glory. An excellent audio guide available in multiple languages brings the cathedral's fascinating history vividly to life.

Visit the museum across the cathedral square to deepen your understanding of Speyer's pivotal role in European history. The collection traces the city's development from Roman times through its prominence in the Holy Roman Empire. In summer, the lively Brezelfest fills the streets with carnival rides and traditional festival tents, while December brings a charming small-scale Christmas market.

activities.text: Stroll along the historic Maximilianstrasse, Germany's oldest and longest pedestrian street

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Experience Stade, a jewel among Germany's Hanseatic cities boasting remarkable maritime charm on the banks of the Schwinge River. This 1,000-year-old trading port features a beautifully preserved old town with half-timbered houses, historic merchant buildings, and the iconic wooden harbor crane. The atmospheric harbor quarter and winding lanes transport visitors back to the glory days of Hanseatic trade.

activities.text: Cycle along the North Sea Cycle Path that winds along the embankments between grazing sheep and the vast coastal landscape. This scenic route offers peaceful riding through traditional fishing villages, past historic lighthouses, and along dikes with panoramic views of the North Sea and Elbe estuary.

Explore Stade's maritime heritage at the harbor where traditional sailing vessels still moor alongside the historic quays. The town's museum in the Swedish Storage House tells the story of centuries of trade, fishing, and seafaring that shaped this Hanseatic gem.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Discover Stendal, proud Roland town of the Altmark, whose monumental statue in the market square symbolizes medieval civic liberties. This Hanseatic city prosperous in the Middle Ages preserves a remarkably homogeneous Gothic brick architectural heritage. Narrow alleyways lead to picturesque squares and hidden treasures revealing the historical importance of this former merchant town.

Marvel at St. Mary's Church which majestically towers above the town. This Gothic brick hall church impresses with its soaring architecture and refined artistic details. St. Jacobi Church, another Gothic jewel, completes this exceptional religious heritage with its stellar vaults and medieval stained glass windows.

Pass through the monumental Uenglingen and Tangermünde gates, impressive witnesses to the town's former fortifications. These Gothic brick gates, among the finest in northern Germany, tell of Stendal's strategic importance in medieval history. The Powder Tower and Renaissance Town Hall complete this remarkable architectural panorama.

activities.text: Visit the Altmark Museum tracing the history of this historic region

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Explore Stolberg, a picturesque town in the southern Harz whose medieval center is miraculously preserved. Historic half-timbered houses lining the steep alleyways create a setting from another time at the foot of the castle overlooking the valley. Stolberg Castle, residence of the eponymous counts, and St. Martin's Church constitute the emblematic symbols of this romantic town.

Discover the birthplace of Thomas Müntzer, radical theologian and leader of the Peasants' War born here in 1489. A statue commemorates this controversial figure who defied the established order in the name of social justice. His revolutionary legacy makes Stolberg an important memorial site for the history of social movements in Europe.

Climb the 200 steps of Joseph's Cross, the world's largest iron double cross erected on the Großer Auerberg. This impressive 38-meter-high metal structure, built in 1896, offers a magnificent view of the Harz massif from its observation platform. The 360-degree panorama rewards the climbing effort with a grandiose natural spectacle.

activities.text: Climb to Joseph's Cross for the panorama over the southern Harz

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: The old town of Stralsund has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2002, jointly with Wismar. It has preserved its medieval urban plan intact, with narrow streets, six large Gothic brick churches, and merchants' houses on parcels typical of the Hanseatic era. Stralsund was one of the most powerful cities of the Hanseatic League in the 14th and 15th centuries, before becoming a Swedish administrative center in the 17th and 18th centuries—Swedish occupation lasted until 1815 and left traces in the architecture and coat of arms.

The Alter Markt, the old market square, is dominated by the Gothic Rathaus, whose origins date back to around 1250. Built in four wings forming a 30-by-60-meter rectangle, it's one of the most remarkable Gothic secular buildings in northern Germany. Its northern façade, adorned with the coats of arms of Hanseatic cities—Stralsund, Hamburg, Lübeck, Rostock, Greifswald—recalls the commercial power of the Hanse. Just beside it, St. Nicholas Church, consecrated in 1276, is the city's oldest church: its baroque lantern spire, added in 1667 after a fire, and its medieval astronomical clock make it an essential stop.

The Ozeaneum, opened in 2008 in Stralsund's harbor, is Germany's largest maritime museum. Its giant aquariums recreate the ecosystems of the North Sea and the Baltic—the largest tank holds millions of liters of seawater. Outside in the harbor, the Gorch Fock I, a training ship launched in 1933, is now a museum vessel moored at the quay.

activities.text: Evening walk in Stralsund harbor: the quays overlook the Strelasund, an arm of the Baltic that separates the city from the island of Rügen. The view across the water with the Gothic spires in the background is characteristic of Pomerania's maritime atmosphere. In summer, short cruises to Rügen or Hiddensee depart from the harbor.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Wander through Tangermünde, considered one of the most beautiful small towns in Germany. This picturesque town at the confluence of the Elbe and Tanger rivers captivates with its cobblestone streets lined with historic half-timbered houses and impressive red brick buildings. The preserved medieval atmosphere instantly transports you several centuries back in time.

Contemplate the imposing medieval Tangermünde Castle, former imperial residence of Charles IV in the 14th century. This fortress overlooking the Elbe offers spectacular views of the town and river banks. The massive ramparts and watchtowers testify to the strategic importance of this town in the history of the Holy Roman Empire.

Admire the Gothic brick Town Hall, an architectural gem of northern Germany with its characteristic stepped gables. St. Stephen's Church, an impressive 14th-century brick building, dominates the town's skyline with its slender bell tower. The Owl Tower (Eulenturm) is among the remarkably preserved remains of the medieval fortifications.

activities.text: Walk along the medieval ramparts with panoramic views over the Elbe
Discover the statue of Grete Minde, a legendary figure of the town

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Savor the beauty of Tegernsee, a resort lake prized by Bavarian aristocracy for centuries. Nestled at the foot of the Alps in a setting of wooded mountains, this lake with pure waters hosts elegant towns with geranium-bedecked houses on its shores. The refined atmosphere and preserved setting make it a destination of choice for Munich residents seeking nature.

Visit the former Benedictine Abbey of Tegernsee, founded in the 8th century, whose monastic brewery perpetuates a millennium-old tradition. The Baroque church, remodeled in the 17th century, houses remarkable artistic treasures. The cloister garden offers peaceful views of the lake and surrounding peaks.

Ascend to the summit of Wallberg by panoramic cable car for a 360-degree view of the Bavarian Alps. At 1,722 meters altitude, the panorama encompasses Tegernsee in its entirety, the Austrian mountain ranges and on clear days, as far as Munich. A panoramic terrace allows you to admire this spectacle in any season.

activities.text: Lakeside walk and discovery of the villages of Bad Wiessee and Rottach-Egern

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Ascend to the Hexentanzplatz, the legendary witches' dance floor, accessible by a panoramic cable car soaring over the Bode Valley. This rocky plateau at 454 meters elevation is the scene of numerous legends surrounding the Harz witches who allegedly gather here every Walpurgis Night. The plunging view over the wild Bode Gorge and surrounding forests creates a panorama of striking beauty.

Climb the Roßtrappe, a 400-meter granite rock offering a fantastic view of the Harz Mountains. According to legend, the horseshoe-shaped imprint visible in the rock comes from Princess Brunhilde's horse, which leapt across the gorge to escape the knight Bodo. The latter, having fallen into the abyss, allegedly gave his name to the Bode River that winds 200 meters below.

Hike on the Harzer Hexenstieg, the famous hiking trail following the Bode River through a spectacular gorge. The Devil's Bridge spans the tumultuous waters in an enchanted forest setting worthy of the Brothers Grimm fairy tales. St. Peter's Church and Goethe's Rock, which the writer visited during his travels in the Harz, complete this immersion in a German romantic landscape.

activities.text: Hike along the Bode on the Hexenstieg trail through the wild gorge

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Relax at Titisee, one of the Black Forest's most beloved lakes nestled in a picture-perfect mountain setting. This natural glacial lake offers crystal-clear waters perfect for swimming, pedal boating or simply strolling along the scenic lakeside promenade. The surrounding forested hills and traditional Black Forest villages create postcard views in every direction.

Browse the shops along the lakefront for authentic Black Forest souvenirs, including the region's famous cuckoo clocks crafted by local clockmakers using traditional techniques. The mechanical artistry of these hand-carved timepieces represents generations of Black Forest craftsmanship passed down through families. Look for shops offering demonstrations of how these intricate clocks are assembled.

Take a boat ride across the glittering waters, either paddling yourself or aboard one of the tourist vessels that cruise the lake. The surrounding mountains are laced with hiking trails offering spectacular views down to the lake and across the Black Forest peaks. A visit to the Menzenschwand valley nearby reveals traditional villages and the famous Café zum Kuckuck.

activities.text: Rent a pedal boat or rowboat to explore the lake at your own pace and enjoy the mountain scenery

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: En route to Triberg, morning stop at Unteruhldingen for the Pfahlbaumuseum (Stilt House Museum), Germany's oldest open-air museum, founded in 1922. Twenty-three Neolithic and Bronze Age dwellings (from 4000 to 850 BC) have been reconstructed on stilts above Lake Constance, based on underwater excavations. The site is part of the 111 prehistoric pile dwelling sites around the Alps, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011. Walkways cross the lake village; the reconstructed houses contain life scenes with mannequins, tools, and period domestic animals.

The Triberg Waterfalls are Germany's highest waterfalls outside the Alpine zone, with a total height of 163 meters across seven distinct tiers. The Gutach River cascades over granite rocks amid a conifer forest. Three marked trails allow the ascent (between 45 minutes and 1.5 hours). The site is illuminated every evening until 10pm.

The Schwarzwaldmuseum in Triberg, built in 1873, presents Black Forest traditional life across 1,600 m²: cuckoo clock manufacturing, regional costumes (including the Bollenhut with red pompoms for married women or black for unmarried), mining life, and one of Europe's largest collections of barrel organs. Triberg has claimed its position as the historic capital of Black Forest clockmaking since the 18th century—the first mechanical cuckoo clocks were manufactured here.

activities.text: Hike on one of the marked trails around the Triberg Waterfalls. The cascade path (Kaskadenweg, ~45 min) follows the successive tiers from bottom to top. Squirrels and uncommon forest bird species are frequently observed along the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Begin with the Porta Nigra, built from 169-170 AD as the northern gate of the Roman city Augusta Treverorum. Its 7,200 sandstone blocks, weighing up to 6 tons each, are assembled without mortar, held by iron clamps sealed with lead. It owes its name to the dark color the stone has taken on over the centuries. If it survived, it's thanks to the hermit Simeon who settled there in the 11th century: Bishop Poppo transformed the gate into a church in his honor in 1040, thus sparing it from demolition. Napoleon had the medieval additions removed during his visit in 1804.

Head to the Basilica of Constantine (Konstantinbasilika), the former imperial throne room—the largest intact building from antiquity: 67 meters long, 27 wide, 33 high. Trier was one of the largest cities in the Roman Empire, called the "Second Rome" in the late 3rd century. Constantine the Great resided here for six years in the early 4th century. The basilica's acoustics, with a 7-second echo, make it a concert venue today.

Continue to St. Peter's Cathedral, Germany's oldest, built in the 4th century on the site of a palace from Constantine's era. The cathedral-Liebfrauenkirche ensemble has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986. The Liebfrauenkirche, completed around 1260, is the first entirely Gothic church in Germany.

Visit the Imperial Baths (Kaiserthermen), begun under Constantine in the early 4th century. They were meant to be the largest baths in the Roman Empire but were never completed—the building was converted into military barracks, then used as a quarry. Descend into the underground passages where slaves fed the hypocausts (underfloor heating systems).

Trier's amphitheater, built in the 2nd century, could accommodate 20,000 spectators—one of the best-preserved Roman amphitheaters north of the Alps. Descend into the underground cellars where gladiators and animals awaited their arena entrance.

Finish with a walk along the Moselle riverbanks and through the old town. Karl Marx was born in Trier in 1818—his birthplace is now a museum. To leave the city, Frankfurt is about 2 hours by train, Luxembourg 50 kilometers away.

activities.text: The Moselle Valley around Trier is one of Germany's most renowned wine regions, specializing in Riesling grown on steep slate slopes. Several estates offer tastings in the surrounding villages.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Fall in love with Tübingen, an enchanting university town that many consider Germany's most beautiful city. The charming old town features a magnificent castle, cobblestone streets lined with half-timbered houses and a picturesque waterfront along the Neckar River. The centuries-old university gives the town a youthful, intellectual energy that fills the winding lanes and historic squares.

Climb to Hohentübingen Castle, perched on a ridge overlooking the town, where university students attend classes in buildings that have educated scholars for over 500 years. The castle courtyard offers panoramic views over the red-tiled roofs and church spires of the old town below. The surrounding vineyards produce local wines that feature on menus throughout the town.

Experience a traditional Stocherkahn boat ride, Tübingen's version of Venetian gondolas, where flat-bottomed punts glide along the Neckar past willow-draped banks. The annual Stocherkahnrennen boat race is a beloved local tradition featuring costumed students and much hilarity. The Holzmarkt square and surrounding streets come alive on summer evenings with locals enjoying the outdoor café culture.

activities.text: Explore the old town's atmospheric squares and imagine yourself in a Harry Potter film set

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Kochberg Castle is a moated castle (Wasserschloss) built around 1600 in Renaissance style, surrounded by a moat. It's known worldwide for its connection to Goethe and Charlotte von Stein. The von Stein family acquired the estate in 1733. Goethe was a regular guest of Charlotte von Stein from 1775 to 1788 - it was at Kochberg that he met Friedrich Schiller for the first time on September 7, 1788, before inviting him to Jena. He wrote his visit dates in ink on the castle's secretary desk, still visible today. More than 1,700 letters from Goethe to Charlotte von Stein testify to this thirteen-year relationship. The museum reconstructs the period's apartments with original furniture, portraits, and manuscript documents.

The six-hectare landscape park was transformed by Carl von Stein (Charlotte's son) between 1800 and 1840, from a Baroque garden into a Romantic English park. Under imposing old trees, winding paths lead to a canvas hut, a tower ruin, a grotto with burial site, a bathing lake, and the flower garden with its floral staircase.

The Liebhabertheater is a 75-seat private theater built around 1800 by Carl von Stein after the model of Weimar's court theater. It's a unique jewel worldwide: the classical columned portico, the hall with its hand-marbled wallpapers and floral friezes, the stage and pit are fully restored. It belongs to the «European Route of Historic Theatres» and hosts every weekend from May to October operas, chamber concerts, and theatrical performances.

activities.text: Performance at Kochberg's Liebhabertheater: Baroque opera or chamber concert in this 75-seat theater, in the authentic atmosphere of the 18th century. Productions are conceived specifically for this space and employ historically informed performance practices.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

places.name: Weimar

visits.text: Tiefurt Palace is a small summer residence on the banks of the Ilm east of Weimar. Originally a modest manor from the mid-16th century enlarged in 1765, it became Duchess Anna Amalia's favorite retreat from 1781 until her death in 1807. Despite its seven rooms per floor, it hosted literary and musical soirées bringing together Goethe, Wieland, and Corona Schröter, making Tiefurt a pastoral «Museion». The music room and the duchess's apartments are restored to their late 18th-century state with original furniture. Tiefurt is part of the UNESCO site «Classical Weimar».

The Bauhaus was founded in Weimar on April 12, 1919, by architect Walter Gropius, who merged the city's Academy of Fine Arts and School of Applied Arts. His manifesto proclaimed that «the ultimate goal of all creative activity is building». Among the recruited masters: Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, László Moholy-Nagy, and Oskar Schlemmer. The school left Weimar in 1925 under pressure from a conservative regional government and moved to Dessau, before being closed by the Nazis in 1933. The Bauhaus Museum Weimar, opened in 2019 for the centenary, holds the world's oldest Bauhaus collection - started by Gropius himself - with 13,000 objects spread across three floors. The Bauhaus sites in Weimar have been UNESCO World Heritage Sites since 1996.

Goethe's House at Frauenplan is the residence the poet occupied for 50 years, from 1782 to his death in 1832. It is preserved as he left it: his study with the mineral collection carefully arranged on shelves, his 5,000 volumes, the bed in which he died. It's the world's most important museum dedicated to Goethe. The house is part of the UNESCO site «Classical Weimar» inscribed in 1998.

Schiller's House is the dwelling the poet occupied during his last three years of life, from 1802 to his death in 1805. It's here that «The Bride of Messina» and «William Tell» were written. The study is preserved with the inclined desk at which Schiller worked standing, his cane, the manuscripts. The modest rooms contrast with the grandeur of the works born there.

The Duchess Anna Amalia Library is housed in the Renaissance «Green Palace» (1562-1565) converted into a library in 1761. Goethe was its director from 1797 to 1832 and doubled the holdings to reach 80,000 volumes. The three-story oval Rococo hall, in wood, is one of Germany's most photographed interiors. On September 2, 2004, a fire destroyed 50,000 volumes and damaged 118,000 - a national tragedy. After restoration, the library reopened in 2007. It preserves the world's largest collection of Goethe's Faust.

The House of the Weimar Republic (Haus der Weimarer Republik), opened in 2019, traces the history of Germany's first democracy. In 1919, the National Constituent Assembly met in Weimar's German National Theater - Berlin was deemed too dangerous amid revolution - and adopted the Constitution of August 11, 1919, instituting universal suffrage and social rights pioneering for the era. The permanent exhibition illuminates the birth, crises, and fall of this Republic.

activities.text: Stroll through Tiefurt's English landscape park along the Ilm River. Open meadows, century-old trees, and small monuments punctuate the main avenue: an artificial grotto, a temple of the muses, a Japanese-style wooden bridge. The park was designed in the spirit of 18th-century «natural» English gardens.

Bauhaus walking tour through Weimar's streets to discover buildings linked to the school: the Haus am Horn (1923), a Bauhaus housing prototype built for an exhibition, and Henry van de Velde's buildings where the school had its first workshops. Specialized guides offer commented tours.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders
Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

map_keywords: Goethe Gartenhaus Park an der Ilm Weimar

visits.text: Contemplate the impressive half-timbered Town Hall of Wernigerode, one of the most photographed in Germany with its pointed turrets and medieval sculptures. This pearl of the Harz captivates with its ensemble of perfectly preserved half-timbered houses, including the town's smallest house that attracts the curious. The neo-Gothic castle enthroned on its hill offers a fairytale silhouette overlooking the colorful rooftops of the old town.

Visit Wernigerode Castle, residence of the Counts of Stolberg-Wernigerode transformed into a museum tracing 19th-century aristocratic life. The sumptuous apartments and decorative art collections testify to the refinement of German nobility. The panoramic view from the terraces embraces the Harz forests and surrounding peaks.

Discover the Steinerne Renne, a spectacular gorge of the Holtemme River where water cascades between enormous moss-covered granite boulders. This natural wonder at the gates of Wernigerode offers a refreshing hike through preserved primeval forest. At the Baumkuchenhaus, this traditional spit-roasted cake perpetuates a unique pastry-making tradition.

activities.text: Excursion to the Schierke district, home of the Schierker Feuerstein herbal liqueur
Visit Pullman City Harz theme park in Hasselfelde, Germany's largest western town

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: The Kurhaus of Wiesbaden is the city's emblem. Inaugurated in 1907 under the impetus of Wilhelm II—who made Wiesbaden his spring residence and actively contributed to its development—this neoclassical building with its colonnade houses a casino (one of Germany's oldest, said to have inspired Dostoevsky's novel The Gambler), twelve salons, and a grand concert hall. It opens onto the Kurpark, landscaped in the 19th century in English style with a lake and fountains.

The Hessisches Staatstheater of Wiesbaden, inaugurated in 1894 in neo-Baroque style, is considered one of Germany's most beautiful theaters. It hosts opera, ballet, and drama. The Maifestspiele festival—founded in 1896 for Wilhelm II's visits—attracts internationally renowned opera productions each year.

Ascend the Neroberg summit (245 m) via the Nerobergbahn, a water-powered funicular inaugurated in 1888—one of the oldest still operating in Germany. At the summit, the Russian Orthodox Church of Saint Elizabeth hides among the trees with its five golden domes. It was built between 1897 and 1899 by Duke Adolf of Nassau in memory of his wife Elisabeth Romanova, daughter of Grand Duke Michael of Russia, who died in childbirth at age 19 on January 27, 1845. The princess's coffin still rests there. The view from the Monopteros—a temple-shaped belvedere—extends across the entire city and Rhine valley.

The Museum Wiesbaden, founded in 1819, brings together modern and contemporary art collections under one roof—including one of the world's largest collections of works by Alexej von Jawlensky, the Expressionist painter who lived in Wiesbaden—along with natural history collections featuring pieces from the Duchy of Nassau.

The Rheingau vineyards stretch across approximately 3,100 hectares between the Taunus and the Rhine's northern bank, around Rüdesheim am Rhein (35 km west of Wiesbaden). Riesling has been cultivated here since the Middle Ages—the first Johannisberg vineyard dates to 817. The south-facing slopes overlooking the Rhine and the thermal shadow of the Taunus create an exceptional microclimate. In Rüdesheim, the Niederwald cable car leads to the Germania statue, erected in 1883 to commemorate the founding of the German Empire.

The Upper Middle Rhine Valley, between Bingen (opposite Rüdesheim) and Koblenz, has been a World Heritage Site since 2002 for its cultural landscapes of vineyards, castles, and villages. Along 65 km, the river corridor is dotted with rocky outcrops, medieval ruins, and terraced slopes—a classic route that can be explored on foot, by bicycle, or by boat.

activities.text: Wine tasting of Rheingau wines at one of the wine bars in Wiesbaden's center—the city itself lies within the appellation. Riesling represents 80% of production; Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir) occupies most of the remaining parcels.

The Rheingauer Weinwoche (Rheingau Wine Week) takes place every year in early August on Wiesbaden's Schlossplatz. Over 100 winemakers present their wines on the castle forecourt. It's one of Hesse's oldest and most popular wine festivals.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Marvel at Wörlitz Park, one of Germany's most beautiful landscape gardens and jewel of the Dessau-Wörlitz Garden Realm, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This 18th-century gardening masterpiece spans 112 hectares of canals, artificial islands, and romantic vistas. Neoclassical pavilions, picturesque bridges, and Greek temples create an idealized landscape inspired by Prince Leopold III's travels to Italy and England.

Visit Wörlitz Palace, Germany's first neoclassical building, whose refined interiors house remarkable art and antiquities collections. St. Peter's Church, harmoniously integrated into the park landscape, completes this architectural ensemble of exceptional aesthetic coherence. Gondolas gliding along the canals offer a unique perspective on this earthly paradise designed according to Enlightenment ideals.

activities.text: Boat ride along the park canals for a romantic perspective

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Autostadt is a 69-acre theme park opened in 2000, built along the Mittelland Canal for the Hanover World Expo. It showcases the Volkswagen Group's brands in carefully designed pavilions. Its most spectacular attraction: two 48-meter glass towers, fully automated, storing up to 800 new vehicles connected to the factory by a 700-meter tunnel. Nearly 175,000 customers come here each year to collect their cars. The ZeitHaus museum traces automotive history from the Ford Model T to the Beetle.
The Kunstmuseum Wolfsburg, inaugurated in 1994, is one of northern Germany's most active contemporary art museums, featuring major temporary exhibitions in a building designed by Peter Schweger.

Wolfsburg Castle is first mentioned in 1302—a Wasserburg, or moated castle, former residence of the noble Bartensleben family. When the British renamed the town in May 1945, they chose this Weser Renaissance castle to erase the name "Stadt des KdF-Wagens" imposed by Hitler in 1938.

activities.text: Walk along the Mittelland Canal with an overview of the Volkswagen facilities: the chimneys of the factory founded in 1938 and the two glass delivery towers form a recognizable industrial skyline.

visits.text: Admire the Würzburg Residence, one of Europe's most beautiful Baroque palaces, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This architectural masterpiece commands respect from the grand ceremonial staircase, covered by the world's largest fresco painted by Tiepolo depicting the four continents. The 340 sumptuously decorated rooms rival Versailles in magnificence.

Explore Würzburg, an elegant city in the heart of Bavarian Franconia wine country. Dominated by Marienberg Fortress perched on its hill, this episcopal city abounds with Baroque churches with dazzling interiors. The Old Main Bridge, adorned with monumental statues of saints, offers a romantic view of the terraced vineyards.

Discover the renowned Franconia vineyards producing excellent white wine served in the characteristic flat bottles called Bocksbeutel. The wine region stretches along the Main River in a hilly landscape punctuated by picturesque wine villages and castles overlooking the hillsides.

activities.text: Walk across the Old Bridge and Franconian wine tasting

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: The Würzburg Residence, built between 1720 and 1744 under the patronage of the Schönborn prince-bishops, stands as Europe's most harmonious Baroque palace according to UNESCO, which inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1981. Architect Balthasar Neumann, just 33 years old at the time, directed construction with assistance from specialists brought in from Vienna, Paris, Italy, and Flanders. The grand staircase houses the world's largest ceiling fresco painted in a single piece (32 × 19 meters), created by Venetian master Tiepolo in just 210 days between 1752 and 1753: it depicts the four known continents paying homage to the prince-bishop. The prince-bishop had initially hired a Milanese artist, but his work proved so poor that Tiepolo was summoned with a promise of 10,000 florins—three times his Venetian salary.

Step through the doors of the Hofkirche, the Residence chapel tucked into the southwest wing. Despite its modest dimensions, it's considered one of the most perfect achievements of 18th-century sacred architecture. Five oval domes rise in succession above twisted pink marble columns crowned with gilded Corinthian capitals. Tiepolo painted the altarpieces here, while Antonio Bossi crafted the stuccowork. After the chapel, stroll through the Court Gardens (Hofgarten), laid out in terraces between 1765 and 1780, adorned with sculptures, putti, and symmetrical flower beds—they too are part of the UNESCO listing.

The Alte Mainbrücke, Würzburg's oldest bridge, has connected the city to Marienberg Fortress since the 15th century. An earlier stone bridge existed as early as 1133, but floods in 1342 and 1442 destroyed it. The current bridge, stretching 185 meters across eight limestone arches, was reconstructed between 1476 and 1703. Since the 1720s, twelve Baroque sandstone statues standing 4.5 meters tall have adorned the pillars: you'll recognize Saint Kilian, the city's patron saint martyred in 689, and Charlemagne. Until 1886, this was the only way to cross the Main at Würzburg. Now pedestrianized, the bridge perpetuates a beloved local tradition: residents gather here at day's end to drink glasses of Franconian Silvaner, the region's signature grape varietal, served in the distinctive flat Bocksbeutel bottles.

St. Kilian's Cathedral (Dom), Germany's fourth-largest Romanesque church by dimension, has dominated the urban landscape since the 11th century. It bears the name of the city's patron saint, an Irish missionary martyred in 689 along with his companions Colman and Totnan. Inside, works by sculptor Tilman Riemenschneider stand alongside Ottonian elements from the early 11th century and Gothic additions. The Schönborn family's funerary chapel, designed by Balthasar Neumann in 1736, can be visited in the transept.

Marienberg Fortress, perched on the left bank of the Main, served as the residence of Würzburg's prince-bishops for five centuries before the Residence was built. Its origins trace back to 706 CE, when a first church was erected on the site—one of the oldest in Germany. The current fortifications blend medieval walls with Renaissance bastions. Inside, the Museum of Franconia (Mainfränkisches Museum) houses the world's largest collection of sculptures by Tilman Riemenschneider, master of the late Gothic period who worked in Würzburg between 1483 and 1531, whose faces carved from limewood achieve a striking expressiveness.

Climb the 247 steps of the Stations of the Cross leading to Käppele, the Pilgrimage Church of the Visitation, built by Balthasar Neumann between 1748 and 1750 atop Nikolausberg hill. It all began in 1640, when a Main River fisherman placed a pietà in his vineyard during the Thirty Years' War; miraculous healings were reported years later, drawing pilgrims. The Stations of the Cross, Germany's largest, is punctuated by 14 chapels containing 77 life-size statues sculpted by Johann Peter Wagner. The church's Rococo interior is adorned with frescoes by Matthäus Günther. The church miraculously survived the March 16, 1945 bombing that destroyed 90% of the city—a German soldier reportedly pretended to be out of ammunition to avoid shelling it.

The Juliusspital, a hospital founded in 1576 by Prince-Bishop Julius Echter von Mespelbrunn "for the indigent and the sick," remains operational today. It derives its income from about a hundred hectares of vineyards it has owned since the 16th century, making it one of Würzburg's three great historic wine estates alongside the Bürgerspital (founded by wealthy burghers). The arcaded inner courtyard, the 17th-century buildings restored after the war, and the bistro where you can taste Würzburger Stein are worth a stop.

On the market square, the Marienkapelle (St. Mary's Chapel) is a Gothic church recognizable by its white and red sandstone façade. Across from it, the Falkenhaus, with its magnificent yellow and white Rococo façade adorned with stucco, houses the tourist office. Franconia's first Baroque collegiate church, St. John im Haug (1670), designed by Italian architect Antonio Petrini, stands out with its two towers flanking a tall façade and a powerful dome—a Crucifixion by Tintoretto (1583) surmounts the high altar.

activities.text: Settle onto the Alte Mainbrücke in late afternoon to savor a glass of Franconian Silvaner as the sun sets over Marienberg Fortress and the vineyard-clad hills—a tradition locals celebrate each summer during the Brückenweinfest, the bridge wine festival.

Descend into the vaulted cellars of the Bürgerspital or Juliusspital for a tasting of Franconian wines. The Franconia wine region, one of Germany's 13 wine-growing regions, is distinguished by its dry white wines—Silvaner, Bacchus, Müller-Thurgau—served in the flat Bocksbeutel bottle, exclusive to Franconia. The terraced vineyards lining the Main and overlooking the city form an integral part of the landscape.
